abstract: We introduce MinerU2.5, a 1.2B-parameter document parsing vision-language model that achieves state-of-the-art recognition accuracy while maintaining exceptional computational efficiency. Our approach employs a coarse-to-fine, two-stage parsing strategy that decouples global layout analysis from local content recognition. In the first stage, the model performs efficient layout analysis on downsampled images to identify structural elements, circumventing the computational overhead of processing high-resolution inputs. In the second stage, guided by the global layout, it performs targeted content recognition on native-resolution crops extracted from the original image, preserving fine-grained details in dense text, complex formulas, and tables. To support this strategy, we developed a comprehensive data engine that generates diverse, large-scale training corpora for both pretraining and fine-tuning. Ultimately, MinerU2.5 demonstrates strong document parsing ability, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, surpassing both general-purpose and domain-specific models across various recognition tasks, while maintaining significantly lower computational overhead.

keyInsights: Decoupling layout analysis at a lower resolution from content recognition at native resolution drastically reduces computational cost while preserving fine-grained details for accuracy.

Systematic identification of 'hard cases' through inference consistency and subsequent human annotation is crucial for building robust models that generalize well to diverse, challenging document types.
Specialized intermediate representations like OTSL for tables and decomposition methods like ADR for formulas can simplify complex parsing tasks and improve VLM performance.
originalProblem: Existing end-to-end Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are computationally inefficient for high-resolution documents due to quadratic complexity of visual tokens and token redundancy.
General-purpose VLMs often suffer from hallucination and lack interpretability when processing dense text in long documents.
Traditional pipeline-based methods for document parsing are prone to error propagation and struggle with robustness across complex, diverse layouts.
results: MinerU2.5 achieved an overall score of 90.67 on OmniDocBench, surpassing previous state-of-the-art models by a notable margin, with top performance in text, formula, and table recognition.
The model demonstrated exceptional efficiency, processing documents at 2.12 pages/s on an A100 GPU, which is 4x faster than MonkeyOCR-pro-3B and 7x faster than dots.ocr.
It established new state-of-the-art for layout analysis using the PageIoU metric and significantly improved formula recognition with a CDM score of 88.46, leading in 4 out of 7 datasets.
solution: MinerU2.5 introduces a decoupled, coarse-to-fine, two-stage parsing strategy within a single 1.2B-parameter VLM, separating global layout analysis from local content recognition.
A comprehensive Data Engine, featuring an Iterative Mining via Inference Consistency (IMIC) strategy, generates diverse, high-quality training data by identifying and prioritizing 'hard cases'.
Task reformulations include an Atomic Decomposition & Recombination (ADR) framework for complex formulas and Optimized Table Structure Language (OTSL) for efficient table recognition.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Lab, Peking University, and Shanghai Jiao Tong University developed MinerU2.5, a decoupled Vision-Language Model that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy for high-resolution document parsing while maintaining exceptional computational efficiency. Its two-stage, coarse-to-fine strategy yields a throughput of 2.12 pages/s on an A100 GPU, outperforming previous models by up to 7x, and secures an overall score of 90.67 on the OmniDocBench benchmark.

title: MinerU2.5: A Decoupled Vision-Language Model for Efficient High-Resolution Document Parsing

abstract: We introduce MonkeyOCR, a document parsing model that advances the state of the art by leveraging a Structure-Recognition-Relation (SRR) triplet paradigm. This design simplifies what would otherwise be a complex multi-tool pipeline and avoids the inefficiencies of processing full pages with giant end-to-end models. In SRR, document parsing is abstracted into three fundamental questions - ``Where is it?'' (structure), ``What is it?'' (recognition), and ``How is it organized?'' (relation) - corresponding to structure detection, content recognition, and relation prediction. To support this paradigm, we present MonkeyDoc, a comprehensive dataset with 4.5 million bilingual instances spanning over ten document types, which addresses the limitations of existing datasets that often focus on a single task, language, or document type. Leveraging the SRR paradigm and MonkeyDoc, we trained a 3B-parameter document foundation model. We further identify parameter redundancy in this model and propose contiguous parameter degradation (CPD), enabling the construction of models from 0.6B to 1.2B parameters that run faster with acceptable performance drop. MonkeyOCR achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing previous open-source and closed-source methods, including Gemini 2.5-Pro. Additionally, the model can be efficiently deployed for inference on a single RTX 3090 GPU. Code and models will be released at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: MonkeyOCR: Document Parsing with a Structure-Recognition-Relation Triplet Paradigm

abstract:
Document parsing is essential for converting unstructured and semi-structured
documents such as contracts, academic papers, and invoices into structured,
machine-readable data. Document parsing reliable structured data from
unstructured inputs, providing huge convenience for numerous applications.
Especially with recent achievements in Large Language Models, document parsing
plays an indispensable role in both knowledge base construction and training
data generation. This survey presents a comprehensive review of the current
state of document parsing, covering key methodologies, from modular pipeline
systems to end-to-end models driven by large vision-language models. Core
components such as layout detection, content extraction (including text,
tables, and mathematical expressions), and multi-modal data integration are
examined in detail. Additionally, this paper discusses the challenges faced by
modular document parsing systems and vision-language models in handling complex
layouts, integrating multiple modules, and recognizing high-density text. It
outlines future research directions and emphasizes the importance of developing
larger and more diverse datasets.

keyInsights: Deep learning, particularly CNNs and Transformers, has become the dominant methodology, driving substantial improvements in accuracy and efficiency across all document parsing sub-tasks.

The field shows a clear trend towards multimodal integration and the emergence of end-to-end Vision-Language Models, which promise more comprehensive and unified document understanding by processing both visual and textual information.

Despite advancements, significant challenges persist in handling complex document layouts, dense and diverse text, intricate mathematical expressions, and optimizing the resource efficiency and fine-grained performance of large VLMs.
originalProblem: The increasing volume and diversity of electronic documents necessitate methods to transform unstructured or semi-structured information into machine-readable, structured data.

Existing document parsing surveys are often outdated, lack comprehensiveness, or focus narrowly on specific sub-technologies, failing to capture recent advancements propelled by deep learning and large language models.
Researchers and practitioners require a consolidated, up-to-date resource that maps the progress, challenges, and future trajectory of document parsing to guide research and practical applications.
results: Deep learning models have fundamentally advanced document parsing, significantly improving accuracy and efficiency across sub-tasks from layout analysis to content extraction.

End-to-end Vision-Language Models, such as Nougat, Fox, Vary, and GOT, demonstrate capabilities in direct conversion to structured formats and handling diverse content, marking a paradigm shift in document processing.

The survey identifies specific limitations for both modular pipelines (e.g., modular coordination, complex layouts, table intricacy) and VLMs (e.g., performance gaps in fine-grained tasks, resource inefficiency), outlining critical areas for future research.
solution: The paper provides a structured taxonomy, analyzing document parsing methodologies by primarily dividing them into traditional modular pipeline systems and modern end-to-end approaches utilizing large vision-language models (VLMs).

It systematically details the key stages within modular pipelines, covering foundational layout analysis and various content extraction techniques for text, mathematical expressions, tables, and charts.

The survey also highlights the paradigm shift towards end-to-end VLMs, describing their evolution and capabilities in unified processing of visual and textual data, bypassing the complexities of multi-stage pipelines.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a comprehensive review of document parsing, categorizing methodologies into modular pipelines and end-to-end vision-language models while detailing their advancements, persistent challenges, and future directions for structured data extraction from diverse documents. It addresses gaps in existing, outdated surveys by incorporating recent developments in deep learning and large language models.

title: Document Parsing Unveiled: Techniques, Challenges, and Prospects for  Structured Information Extraction

abstract:
Document image parsing is challenging due to its complexly intertwined
elements such as text paragraphs, figures, formulas, and tables. Current
approaches either assemble specialized expert models or directly generate
page-level content autoregressively, facing integration overhead, efficiency
bottlenecks, and layout structure degradation despite their decent performance.
To address these limitations, we present \textit{Dolphin}
(\textit{\textbf{Do}cument Image \textbf{P}arsing via \textbf{H}eterogeneous
Anchor Prompt\textbf{in}g}), a novel multimodal document image parsing model
following an analyze-then-parse paradigm. In the first stage, Dolphin generates
a sequence of layout elements in reading order. These heterogeneous elements,
serving as anchors and coupled with task-specific prompts, are fed back to
Dolphin for parallel content parsing in the second stage. To train Dolphin, we
construct a large-scale dataset of over 30 million samples, covering
multi-granularity parsing tasks. Through comprehensive evaluations on both
prevalent benchmarks and self-constructed ones, Dolphin achieves
state-of-the-art performance across diverse page-level and element-level
settings, while ensuring superior efficiency through its lightweight
architecture and parallel parsing mechanism. The code and pre-trained models
are publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: Decomposing document parsing into a two-stage 'analyze-then-parse' paradigm, starting with global layout analysis and followed by element-level content parsing, improves both efficiency and accuracy.
Parallel processing of individual elements, guided by type-specific prompts (heterogeneous anchor prompting), significantly boosts parsing speed while maintaining high recognition quality.
Using cropped element regions ('local views') for fine-grained content parsing is more effective than relying solely on global document context.
originalProblem: Documents contain a highly complex mix of heterogeneous elements (text, figures, formulas, tables) that are structurally and semantically intertwined, making accurate extraction challenging.
Existing integration-based models suffer from high integration overhead and coordination issues among specialized components.

Autoregressive Vision-Language Models (VLMs) experience efficiency bottlenecks for long documents, often lead to layout structure degradation, and sometimes struggle with specialized element recognition.
results: Dolphin achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on complex documents (e.g., 0.1028 Edit Distance on Dolphin-Page, outperforming GPT-4o at 0.3580 ED and Claude3.5-Sonnet at 0.1327 ED).
The model demonstrates exceptional efficiency, processing documents at 0.1729 Frames Per Second (FPS), nearly 2 times faster than the most efficient baseline (Mathpix at 0.0944 FPS).
It shows competitive results on element-level parsing tasks, including high Character Difference Metric (CDM) scores for formulas and promising TEDS scores for tables.
solution: A unified encoder-decoder transformer adopts an "analyze-then-parse" paradigm, first identifying page-level layout elements and reading order (Stage 1), then performing high-precision content recognition for each element (Stage 2).

Stage 2 utilizes parallel processing of cropped element regions combined with heterogeneous anchor prompting, where type-specific prompts guide the model to parse content (e.g., HTML for tables, LaTeX for formulas).
The model is trained on a large-scale, multi-granularity dataset with over 30 million samples, covering both page-level documents and isolated element-level components (tables, formulas).
paper_summary.summary: Dolphin, developed by ByteDance, introduces an "analyze-then-parse" paradigm that uses heterogeneous anchor prompting to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy and nearly 2x faster processing for document image parsing. It effectively handles complex layouts and diverse content types by first analyzing global structure and then performing parallel, type-specific content extraction.

title: Dolphin: Document Image Parsing via Heterogeneous Anchor Prompting

abstract:
Document content extraction is a critical task in computer vision,
underpinning the data needs of large language models (LLMs) and
retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. Despite recent progress, current
document parsing methods have not been fairly and comprehensively evaluated due
to the narrow coverage of document types and the simplified, unrealistic
evaluation procedures in existing benchmarks. To address these gaps, we
introduce OmniDocBench, a novel benchmark featuring high-quality annotations
across nine document sources, including academic papers, textbooks, and more
challenging cases such as handwritten notes and densely typeset newspapers.
OmniDocBench supports flexible, multi-level evaluations--ranging from an
end-to-end assessment to the task-specific and attribute--based analysis using
19 layout categories and 15 attribute labels. We conduct a thorough evaluation
of both pipeline-based methods and end-to-end vision-language models, revealing
their strengths and weaknesses across different document types. OmniDocBench
sets a new standard for the fair, diverse, and fine-grained evaluation in
document parsing. Dataset and code are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: General Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate better generalization to unconventional document types and higher robustness to visual degradations than pipeline tools.
Pipeline-based methods excel in handling high-density documents like newspapers and complex multi-column layouts due to their component-wise processing and resolution capabilities.
No single model or paradigm currently achieves optimal performance across all document types, layout complexities, and visual degradation scenarios, highlighting the need for hybrid approaches.
originalProblem: Existing document parsing benchmarks lack diversity, primarily focusing on specific document types like academic papers and neglecting real-world documents such as textbooks, financial reports, and handwritten notes.

Inconsistent and insufficient evaluation metrics hinder fair comparison across models, especially for complex structures like formulas (LaTeX) and tables (HTML/LaTeX) where generic text similarity fails.

Current benchmarks do not provide fine-grained performance analysis, making it difficult to identify model weaknesses at the element level (text, formula), across document types, or under challenging visual attributes (fuzzy scans, watermarks).
results: Pipeline tools (e.g., MinerU, Mathpix) performed strongly on common document types and dense layouts, while general VLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Qwen2-VL) showed better generalization on specialized data like slides and handwritten notes, and higher robustness to visual degradations.
VLMs struggled with densely typeset newspapers due to input resolution and token length limitations, whereas pipeline tools achieved better performance on these documents.

Specialized models like DocLayout-YOLO (for layout detection), PaddleOCR (for text recognition), and GPT-4o/Mathpix/UniMERNet (for formula recognition) generally outperformed general VLMs in their respective single tasks.
solution: Introduces OmniDocBench, a new benchmark dataset comprising 981 diverse PDF pages from 9 distinct document types, spanning various layouts and page-level attributes (e.g., fuzzy scans, watermarks).

Provides comprehensive, multi-level annotations including 19 layout region categories, reading order, affiliation relationships, and content recognition for text, LaTeX formulas, and HTML/LaTeX tables.

Proposes a multi-faceted evaluation methodology with an end-to-end pipeline using specific metrics like Normalized Edit Distance (NED) for text/reading order, Tree-Edit-Distance-based Similarity (TEDS) for tables, and Character Detection Matching (CDM) for formulas, enabling fine-grained analysis.
paper_summary.summary: OmniDocBench establishes a new benchmark for diverse PDF document parsing, offering a dataset of 981 pages across nine document types with comprehensive layout and content annotations. The paper presents a multi-level evaluation framework and reveals that while pipeline tools excel in structured and dense documents, general Vision-Language Models demonstrate superior generalization and robustness to visual degradation.

title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with  Comprehensive Annotations

abstract: We present Multimodal OCR (MOCR), a document parsing paradigm that jointly parses text and graphics into unified textual representations. Unlike conventional OCR systems that focus on text recognition and leave graphical regions as cropped pixels, our method, termed this http URL, treats visual elements such as charts, diagrams, tables, and icons as first-class parsing targets, enabling systems to parse documents while preserving semantic relationships across elements. It offers several advantages: (1) it reconstructs both text and graphics as structured outputs, enabling more faithful document reconstruction; (2) it supports end-to-end training over heterogeneous document elements, allowing models to exploit semantic relations between textual and visual components; and (3) it converts previously discarded graphics into reusable code-level supervision, unlocking multimodal supervision embedded in existing documents. To make this paradigm practical at scale, we build a comprehensive data engine from PDFs, rendered webpages, and native SVG assets, and train a compact 3B-parameter model through staged pretraining and supervised fine-tuning. We evaluate this http URL from two perspectives: document parsing and structured graphics parsing. On document parsing benchmarks, it ranks second only to Gemini 3 Pro on our OCR Arena Elo leaderboard, surpasses existing open-source document parsing systems, and sets a new state of the art of 83.9 on olmOCR Bench. On structured graphics parsing, our model achieves higher reconstruction quality than Gemini 3 Pro across image-to-SVG benchmarks, demonstrating strong performance on charts, UI layouts, scientific figures, and chemical diagrams. These results show a scalable path toward building large-scale image-to-code corpora for multimodal pretraining. Code and models are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Reframing document parsing to include the recovery of information-dense graphics as renderable code (e.g., SVG) provides scalable, executable multimodal supervision.
An end-to-end multimodal architecture integrating a high-resolution vision encoder and an autoregressive language model decoder can effectively handle heterogeneous content.
A staged training recipe, combining diverse pre-training data sources with targeted instruction tuning, enables robust learning across text, layout, and structured visual elements.
originalProblem: Traditional OCR systems discard structural and semantic information from non-textual document elements (e.g., charts, diagrams) by treating them as opaque pixel regions.
This lossy approach to document parsing limits the structured supervision available for training advanced multimodal models and prevents a comprehensive understanding of document content.
Existing methods struggle to unify text and visual information, leading to fragmented interpretations of document knowledge.
results: Achieved an Elo score of 1124.7 on the OCR Arena across multiple benchmarks (olmOCR-Bench, OmniDocBench 1.5, XDocParse), positioning it as the top open-source system for document parsing.
Demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in structured graphics parsing, with `dots.mocr-svg` achieving a 0.902 UniSVG score and outperforming Gemini 3 Pro across multiple image-to-SVG benchmarks.

Maintained competitive performance on general VQA tasks, outperforming models like Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct on CharXiv and showing strong results on DocVQA and ChartQA, indicating robust multimodal reasoning.
solution: Develops Multimodal OCR (MOCR), a unified parsing paradigm that treats both textual and visual document elements as first-class targets.
Converts information-dense graphics (charts, diagrams) into reusable, renderable structured code (SVG), alongside text and layout structures.

Employs a `dots.mocr` architecture comprising a high-resolution vision encoder, multimodal connector, and an autoregressive language model, trained with staged pre-training and instruction tuning on a comprehensive data engine.
paper_summary.summary: The Multimodal OCR (MOCR) system parses diverse document elements, including text and complex graphics, into a unified, ordered sequence of structured representations, such as renderable SVG code for visual components. It achieves an Elo score of 1124.7 on the OCR Arena benchmark and a 0.902 UniSVG score for image-to-SVG parsing, establishing new state-of-the-art performance for open-source systems while also outperforming proprietary models like Gemini 3 Pro on graphic reconstruction tasks.

title: Multimodal OCR: Parse Anything from Documents

abstract: Optical character recognition (OCR) has evolved from line-level transcription to structured document parsing, requiring models to recover long-form sequences containing layout, tables, and formulas. Despite recent advances in vision-language models, most existing systems rely on autoregressive decoding, which introduces sequential latency and amplifies error propagation in long documents. In this work, we revisit document OCR from an inverse rendering perspective, arguing that left-to-right causal generation is an artifact of serialization rather than an intrinsic property of the task. Motivated by this insight, we propose MinerU-Diffusion, a unified diffusion-based framework that replaces autoregressive sequential decoding with parallel diffusion denoising under visual conditioning. MinerU-Diffusion employs a block-wise diffusion decoder and an uncertainty-driven curriculum learning strategy to enable stable training and efficient long-sequence inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MinerU-Diffusion consistently improves robustness while achieving up to 3.2x faster decoding compared to autoregressive baselines. Evaluations on the proposed Semantic Shuffle benchmark further confirm its reduced dependence on linguistic priors and stronger visual OCR capability.

keyInsights: Diffusion-based decoding offers a more theoretically aligned and efficient paradigm for document OCR compared to autoregressive methods due to its deterministic and spatially coupled nature.

Block-attention mechanisms and dynamic confidence-guided scheduling enable substantial inference efficiency improvements and better scalability while mitigating issues like repetition and drift in long sequences.
Reducing dependency on linguistic priors through diffusion decoding significantly enhances robustness to semantic distortions, leading to more visually grounded and reliable OCR outputs.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) decoders in OCR suffer from sequential latency, scaling linearly with document length.
AR's causal generation leads to error propagation, where initial errors cascade through the output.
Over-reliance on linguistic priors in AR models can cause "semantic hallucinations" and reduced robustness when visual signals are ambiguous.
results: Achieved an 88.94 Overall score on OmniDocBench v1.5 without ground truth layout, and 93.37 with ground truth, positioning it competitively with top-tier AR systems.
Demonstrated substantial inference speedups, up to 3.2x faster (164.8 TPS) compared to AR baselines at matched accuracy, and 2.1x faster (108.9 TPS) with 93%+ accuracy.
Exhibited strong robustness on the Semantic Shuffle benchmark, maintaining nearly constant performance under increasing semantic distortion, unlike AR decoders.
solution: Reconceptualizes document OCR as an inverse rendering problem, using a discrete diffusion process for parallel text reconstruction conditioned on visual input.
Introduces MinerU-Diffusion, a block-attention dVLM, which divides the output sequence into blocks for coarse-grained autoregressive decoding between blocks and parallel diffusion within blocks.

Employs a two-stage curriculum learning strategy, starting with diversity-driven foundational training, followed by uncertainty-driven refinement on challenging samples for improved stability and precision.
paper_summary.summary: MinerU-Diffusion re-frames document OCR as an inverse rendering problem, employing a diffusion decoder to achieve up to 3.2 times faster inference with enhanced robustness against semantic distortions compared to autoregressive methods, while maintaining competitive accuracy on full-document parsing benchmarks.

title: MinerU-Diffusion: Rethinking Document OCR as Inverse Rendering via Diffusion Decoding

abstract: Document parsing is a core task in document intelligence, supporting applications such as information extraction, retrieval-augmented generation, and automated document analysis. However, real-world documents often feature complex layouts with multi-level tables, embedded images or formulas, and cross-page structures, which remain challenging for existing OCR systems. We introduce MonkeyOCR v1.5, a unified vision-language framework that enhances both layout understanding and content recognition through a two-stage pipeline. The first stage employs a large multimodal model to jointly predict layout and reading order, leveraging visual information to ensure sequential consistency. The second stage performs localized recognition of text, formulas, and tables within detected regions, maintaining high visual fidelity while reducing error propagation. To address complex table structures, we propose a visual consistency-based reinforcement learning scheme that evaluates recognition quality via render-and-compare alignment, improving structural accuracy without manual annotations. Additionally, two specialized modules, Image-Decoupled Table Parsing and Type-Guided Table Merging, are introduced to enable reliable parsing of tables containing embedded images and reconstruction of tables crossing pages or columns. Comprehensive experiments on OmniDocBench v1.5 demonstrate that MonkeyOCR v1.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming PPOCR-VL and MinerU 2.5 while showing exceptional robustness in visually complex document scenarios. A trial link can be found at this https URL .

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: A two-stage VLM-based framework effectively balances global visual reasoning with localized recognition, offering efficiency without sacrificing accuracy in complex document parsing.
Self-supervised refinement of table structures through visual consistency-based reinforcement learning can significantly improve fidelity and reduce reliance on costly manual annotations.

Specialized, modular solutions like decoupling image content from table structure parsing and employing type-guided merging for fragmented tables are effective for persistent complex document challenges.
originalProblem: Existing OCR systems struggle with complex layouts, intricate multi-level table structures, irregular typography, and diverse languages found in real-world documents.
Traditional pipeline methods suffer from cascading errors, while end-to-end large multimodal models (LMMs) incur massive computational costs for high-resolution document images.

Current systems falter in accurately parsing tables with embedded images, merged cells, multi-level headers, and those spanning multiple pages or columns, often requiring prohibitive manual annotation.
results: Achieved an overall performance score of 93.01% on OmniDocBench v1.5, surpassing PPOCR-VL by 0.15% and MinerU2.5 by 2.34%, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

Demonstrated exceptional robustness in visually complex scenarios, scoring 90.9% on the OCRFlux-complex dataset (9.2% better than PPOCR-VL) and achieving best results on "Newspaper" and "Notes" document types.

Successfully enabled robust parsing of challenging table structures, including accurate restoration of embedded images via IDTP and seamless reconstruction of cross-page/column tables via TGTM, outperforming competitors in preserving structural integrity.
solution: Introduces a streamlined two-stage vision-language framework for joint layout detection and reading order prediction, followed by region-level content recognition, using a single VLM.
Develops a visual consistency-based reinforcement learning paradigm to enhance complex table recognition without extensive manual annotations, guided by a VLM-based reward model.
Proposes Image-Decoupled Table Parsing (IDTP) to handle tables with embedded images and Type-Guided Table Merging (TGTM) to systematically reconstruct tables split across pages or columns.
paper_summary.summary: MonkeyOCR v1.5 presents a unified vision-language framework to robustly parse complex document structures, including intricate multi-level and cross-page tables with embedded images. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on OmniDocBench v1.5 and demonstrates superior accuracy in challenging real-world document scenarios.

title: MonkeyOCR v1.5 Technical Report: Unlocking Robust Document Parsing for Complex Patterns

abstract: This paper presents Extract-0, a 7-billion parameter language model specifically optimized for document information extraction that achieves performance exceeding models with parameter counts several orders of magnitude larger. Through a novel combination of synthetic data generation, supervised fine-tuning with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), and reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), Extract-0 achieves a mean reward of 0.573 on a benchmark of 1,000 diverse document extraction tasks, outperforming GPT-4.1 (0.457), o3 (0.464), and GPT-4.1-2025 (0.459). The training methodology employs a memory-preserving synthetic data generation pipeline that produces 280,128 training examples from diverse document sources, followed by parameterefficient fine-tuning that modifies only 0.53% of model weights (40.4M out of 7.66B parameters). The reinforcement learning phase introduces a novel semantic similarity-based reward function that handles the inherent ambiguity in information extraction tasks. This research demonstrates that task-specific optimization can yield models that surpass general-purpose systems while requiring substantially fewer computational resource.

keyInsights: Task-specific optimization of smaller language models can achieve superior performance over larger, general-purpose LLMs for specialized tasks like document information extraction.
Advanced document extraction capabilities can be developed and deployed with remarkably low computational costs (e.g., $196 total training cost on a single H100 GPU).

A multi-stage training approach combining supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with a semantic similarity-based reward function is highly effective for improving both extraction accuracy and output structure validity.
originalProblem: Document information extraction (DIE) is a pervasive bottleneck across industries, traditionally reliant on costly, error-prone manual or rule-based systems.
Large Language Models (LLMs) are computationally prohibitive for many organizations due to high deployment and inference costs.
General-purpose LLMs often deliver sub-optimal performance for highly specialized and precise structured information extraction tasks.
results: Extract-0 achieved a mean reward of 0.573 on a 1,000-task benchmark, surpassing GPT-4.1 (0.457) by 25% and outperforming other leading generalist LLMs.
The total training cost for Extract-0 amounted to $196 using a single NVIDIA H100 GPU, demonstrating high cost-efficiency.
The model consistently produced valid JSON output for 89% of tasks after reinforcement learning, a substantial increase from the base model's 42.7% validity.
solution: A multi-source, memory-preserving synthetic data generation pipeline created diverse, contextually coherent training examples for various document types.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) was applied to the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B model, adapting only 0.53% of its parameters.

Reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) utilized a custom field-level semantic similarity reward function, incorporating type-aware comparisons for lists, scalars, and strings.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Inteli developed Extract-0, a 7-billion parameter specialized language model optimized for document information extraction, which achieved a 25% performance improvement over GPT-4.1 on a diverse benchmark with a mean reward of 0.573. This model's training process incurred a low total cost of $196, making advanced extraction capabilities more accessible.

title: Extract-0: A Specialized Language Model for Document Information Extraction

abstract: Recent advances in Large Vision-Language models (LVLM) have spurred significant progress in document parsing task. Compared to traditional pipeline-based methods, end-to-end paradigms have shown their excellence in converting PDF images into structured outputs through integrated Optical Character Recognition (OCR), table recognition, mathematical formula recognition and so on. However, the absence of explicit analytical stages for document layouts and reading orders limits the LVLM's capability in handling complex document types such as multi-column newspapers or posters. To address this limitation, we propose in this report Logics-Parsing: an end-to-end LVLM-based model augmented with reinforcement learning. Our model incorporates meticulously designed reward mechanisms to optimize complex layout analysis and reading order inference. In addition, we expand the model's versatility by incorporating diverse data types such as chemical formulas and handwritten Chinese characters into supervised fine-tuning. Finally, to enable rigorous evaluation of our approach, we introduce LogicsParsingBench, a curated set of 1,078 page-level PDF images spanning nine major categories and over twenty sub-categories, which will be released later. Comprehensive experiments conducted on LogicsParsingBench have validated the efficacy and State-of-the-art (SOTA) performance of our proposed model across diverse document analysis scenarios. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: Supervised fine-tuning with a high-quality, diverse dataset is crucial for establishing strong foundational document parsing capabilities before applying reinforcement learning.

A multi-component reward function combining character similarity, localization accuracy, and logical reading flow significantly enhances an LVLM's ability to handle complex document layouts and infer correct reading order.

Rigorous dataset curation, including expert human verification and inference-guided hard-mining, is essential for both foundational training and targeted improvement via reinforcement learning, especially for challenging document structures.
originalProblem: Existing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) struggle with accurately inferring reading order and modeling complex document layouts due to their coarse-grained visual-text alignment and standard next-token prediction objectives.

Current document parsing models lack robustness in recognizing a diverse array of content types, including mathematical formulas, chemical structures, and handwritten characters, within a unified framework.

Previous reinforcement learning approaches for document parsing often lacked precise reward mechanisms for layout and reading order, or were applied without a strong, document-parsing-specific supervised fine-tuning foundation.
results: Logics-Parsing achieved the lowest aggregate edit distance on the LogicsParsingBench for both English (0.124) and Chinese (0.145) documents, surpassing pipeline tools, expert VLMs, and general VLMs.
The LC-RL stage improved the Reading Order Edit distance from 0.149 to 0.136 for English and from 0.155 to 0.113 for Chinese, demonstrating the effectiveness of the reinforcement learning approach.

The model showed particular strength in parsing pure text, chemical structures, and handwritten content, though identified areas for future improvement in table structure and mathematical formula recognition.
solution: A two-stage training paradigm is introduced: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a large, diverse dataset to establish foundational understanding, followed by Layout-Centric Reinforcement Learning (LC-RL) to refine structural and reading order inference.
The model utilizes Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct as its base LVLM and generates structured HTML sequences, integrating public and an extensively human-verified in-house dataset for training.

The LC-RL stage employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a multi-component reward function that evaluates character-level similarity, localization accuracy of bounding boxes, and logical reading flow (pairwise inversion count) to explicitly optimize for layout and reading order.
paper_summary.summary: The Logics-Parsing framework developed by Alibaba Group presents an end-to-end Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM) for structured document parsing, augmented by a two-stage supervised fine-tuning and layout-centric reinforcement learning approach. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on the new LogicsParsingBench benchmark for English (0.124 aggregate edit distance) and Chinese (0.145 aggregate edit distance) documents, demonstrating enhanced reading order inference and robust handling of diverse content types.

title: Logics-Parsing Technical Report

abstract: Document content analysis has been a crucial research area in computer vision. Despite significant advancements in methods such as OCR, layout detection, and formula recognition, existing open-source solutions struggle to consistently deliver high-quality content extraction due to the diversity in document types and content. To address these challenges, we present MinerU, an open-source solution for high-precision document content extraction. MinerU leverages the sophisticated PDF-Extract-Kit models to extract content from diverse documents effectively and employs finely-tuned preprocessing and postprocessing rules to ensure the accuracy of the final results. Experimental results demonstrate that MinerU consistently achieves high performance across various document types, significantly enhancing the quality and consistency of content extraction. The MinerU open-source project is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A multi-stage pipeline, integrating specialized models for distinct tasks like layout, formula, and table extraction, effectively addresses document complexity.
Fine-tuning general-purpose vision models on diverse document datasets yields improved accuracy for layout and formula detection.
Advanced post-processing, such as resolving overlapping bounding boxes and enforcing human reading order, is critical for generating coherent and structured document outputs.
originalProblem: Existing document content extraction methods struggle with complex layouts, mathematical formulas, and tables, often failing to maintain correct reading order.
Traditional OCR and library-based parsers are inadequate for diverse document types and non-text elements.
Multi-module and end-to-end MLLM approaches face challenges with real-world document diversity and high computational costs.
results: MinerU's fine-tuned models outperform several existing open-source solutions in layout and formula detection on academic and multi-source datasets.
Its formula recognition component, UniMERNet, achieves performance comparable to commercial solutions.
The framework successfully extracts content from a wide range of document types, including academic papers, financial reports, textbooks, and exam papers.
solution: MinerU utilizes a four-stage pipeline: preprocessing, content parsing, post-processing, and format conversion.

It incorporates PDF-Extract-Kit, an algorithm library featuring specialized models for layout detection (fine-tuned LayoutLMv3), formula detection (fine-tuned YOLOv8), and formula recognition (UniMERNet).
Advanced post-processing techniques resolve overlapping bounding boxes and segment content for human reading order, also merging cross-column/page paragraphs.
paper_summary.summary: MinerU, an open-source framework from Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, offers high-precision document content extraction by integrating specialized models with sophisticated pre- and post-processing. This system accurately handles complex layouts, mathematical formulas, and tables while preserving logical reading order across various document types like academic papers and financial reports.

title: MinerU: An Open-Source Solution for Precise Document Content Extraction

abstract:
Language models for scientific tasks are trained on text from scientific
publications, most distributed as PDFs that require parsing. PDF parsing
approaches range from inexpensive heuristics (for simple documents) to
computationally intensive ML-driven systems (for complex or degraded ones). The
choice of the "best" parser for a particular document depends on its
computational cost and the accuracy of its output. To address these issues, we
introduce an Adaptive Parallel PDF Parsing and Resource Scaling Engine
(AdaParse), a data-driven strategy for assigning an appropriate parser to each
document. We enlist scientists to select preferred parser outputs and
incorporate this information through direct preference optimization (DPO) into
AdaParse, thereby aligning its selection process with human judgment. AdaParse
then incorporates hardware requirements and predicted accuracy of each parser
to orchestrate computational resources efficiently for large-scale parsing
campaigns. We demonstrate that AdaParse, when compared to state-of-the-art
parsers, improves throughput by $17\times$ while still achieving comparable
accuracy (0.2 percent better) on a benchmark set of 1000 scientific documents.
AdaParse's combination of high accuracy and parallel scalability makes it
feasible to parse large-scale scientific document corpora to support the
development of high-quality, trillion-token-scale text datasets. The
implementation is available at this https URL

keyInsights: No single PDF parser performs optimally across all scientific document types due to their diverse structures and layouts, necessitating an adaptive approach.
Traditional accuracy metrics for text extraction (e.g., BLEU) do not always align with human perception of parsing quality, highlighting the importance of incorporating user preferences.
Efficient resource utilization, including parallel execution and hardware acceleration, is crucial for scaling high-quality PDF parsing to massive scientific document collections.
originalProblem: Existing PDF parsing methods struggle with the diverse structures and complex layouts (e.g., multi-column text, figures, equations) of scientific documents, leading to text extraction errors.
A significant trade-off exists between the quality and speed of PDF parsers, with higher-quality parsers typically requiring more computational resources and running slower.
Scaling PDF parsing to millions of scientific documents for training large language models (LLMs) is challenging due to the computational demands and variability in parser performance.
results: AdaParse achieved 0.2% better accuracy compared to state-of-the-art parsers on a benchmark of 1,000 scientific documents.
The system demonstrated a 17x improvement in throughput compared to high-quality parsers like Nougat while maintaining comparable accuracy.
AdaParse's adaptive approach consistently outperformed any single parsing method in overall quality and efficiency, demonstrating excellent scalability on high-performance computing systems.
solution: AdaParse employs a hierarchical, three-stage classification system (Validation, Improvement, Parser Selection) to intelligently select the most appropriate parser for each PDF document.
It initiates parsing with a fast, efficient parser (PyMuPDF) and then uses text features, metadata, and an LLM-based system to route documents to specialized, higher-quality parsers when needed.
The system incorporates resource optimization techniques like batching, prefetching, parallel execution, and GPU acceleration to achieve high throughput and efficient utilization on HPC systems.
paper_summary.summary: AdaParse is an adaptive engine designed to parse scientific PDF documents at scale, dynamically selecting the optimal parser for each document to balance text extraction quality and computational efficiency. The system achieved 0.2% better accuracy and a 17x higher throughput compared to existing high-quality parsers, enabling the creation of large, high-quality scientific text datasets for training language models.

title: AdaParse: An Adaptive Parallel PDF Parsing and Resource Scaling Engine

abstract: We present FireRed-OCR, a systematic framework to specialize general VLMs into high-performance OCR models. Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive general capabilities but frequently suffer from ``structural hallucination'' when processing complex documents, limiting their utility in industrial OCR applications. In this paper, we introduce FireRed-OCR, a novel framework designed to transform general-purpose VLMs (based on Qwen3-VL) into pixel-precise structural document parsing experts. To address the scarcity of high-quality structured data, we construct a ``Geometry + Semantics'' Data Factory. Unlike traditional random sampling, our pipeline leverages geometric feature clustering and multi-dimensional tagging to synthesize and curate a highly balanced dataset, effectively handling long-tail layouts and rare document types. Furthermore, we propose a Three-Stage Progressive Training strategy that guides the model from pixel-level perception to logical structure generation. This curriculum includes: (1) Multi-task Pre-alignment to ground the model's understanding of document structure; (2) Specialized SFT for standardizing full-image Markdown output; and (3) Format-Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which utilizes reinforcement learning to enforce strict syntactic validity and structural integrity (e.g., table closure, formula syntax). Extensive evaluations on OmniDocBench v1.5 demonstrate that FireRed-OCR achieves state-of-the-art performance with an overall score of 92.94\%, significantly outperforming strong baselines such as DeepSeek-OCR 2 and OCRVerse across text, formula, table, and reading order metrics. We open-source our code and model weights to facilitate the ``General VLM to Specialized Structural Expert'' paradigm.

keyInsights: A dedicated "Geometry + Semantics" Data Factory, incorporating techniques like geometric clustering and expert-model distillation for annotation, is crucial for overcoming data quality and diversity challenges in structural document parsing.

A progressive training curriculum, starting with multi-task pre-alignment using coarser annotations and advancing to specialized supervised fine-tuning, effectively instills structural discipline and reduces initial learning friction in VLMs.

Reinforcement learning via Format-Constrained GRPO, using custom reward functions for formula syntax, hierarchical closure, and table integrity, is an effective mechanism to mitigate "structural hallucination" and achieve precise, rule-governed output generation.
originalProblem: General Vision-Language Models (VLMs) frequently suffer from "structural hallucination" when parsing complex documents, generating outputs that are semantically plausible but structurally incorrect.

Existing document processing methods, including end-to-end OCR models, often lack the fine-grained spatial grounding and strict structural adherence required for industrial-grade document reconstruction.

A shortage of high-quality, structurally diverse document datasets hinders the effective training of VLMs for precise structural understanding, especially for long-tail layouts and complex document types.
results: FireRed-OCR-2B achieved a state-of-the-art overall score of 92.94% on OmniDocBench v1.5, surpassing leading end-to-end specialists and larger general VLMs like Qwen3-VL-235B (89.15%) and Gemini-3.0 Pro (90.33%).

The framework demonstrated substantial improvements over its Qwen3-VL-2B backbone, with an 11.07-point increase in overall score and a 20.54-point increase in Table TEDS on OmniDocBench v1.5, validating the specialization approach.

It secured the lowest Edit Distance (Text_Edit) of 0.032 and the best Reading Order (R-order_Edit) score of 0.041, alongside strong performance in Table TEDS (90.31) and Formula CDM (91.71), demonstrating comprehensive accuracy across document elements.
solution: A "Geometry + Semantics" Data Factory is introduced to create balanced, high-quality datasets by leveraging geometric clustering for rare layout mining, multi-dimensional tagging, stratified sampling, and expert-level refinement via model distillation.
A Three-Stage Progressive Training strategy guides the VLM from basic pixel-level perception to sophisticated logical structure generation, building upon Qwen3-VL as its base.

Format-Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is employed as a reinforcement learning approach with a composite reward function to explicitly enforce strict syntactic validity and structural integrity in generated outputs.
paper_summary.summary: FireRed-OCR, developed by the Super Intelligence Team at Xiaohongshu Inc., specializes general Vision-Language Models for pixel-precise structural document parsing. It achieves state-of-the-art performance with an overall score of 92.94% on OmniDocBench v1.5, significantly outperforming larger general VLMs and end-to-end specialists by effectively mitigating "structural hallucination."

title: FireRed-OCR Technical Report

abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) is fundamental to Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and high-quality data generation for LLM training. Yet, despite progress in average OCR accuracy, state-of-the-art VLMs still struggle with detecting sample-level errors and lack effective unsupervised quality control. We introduce Consensus Entropy (CE), a training-free, model-agnostic metric that estimates output reliability by measuring inter-model agreement entropy. The core insight is that correct predictions converge in output space, while errors diverge. Based on CE, we develop CE-OCR, a lightweight multi-model framework that verifies outputs by ensemble agreement, selects the best outputs, and further improves efficiency through adaptive routing. Experiments demonstrate that CE is robust for quality verification, improving F1 scores by 42.1\% over VLM-as-Judge. CE-OCR achieves consistent OCR gains, outperforming self-consistency and single-model baselines at the same cost. Notably, CE requires no training or supervision, enabling plug-and-play integration.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: Consensus Entropy: Harnessing Multi-VLM Agreement for Self-Verifying and Self-Improving OCR

abstract: Automated parsing of scanned documents into richly structured, machine-readable formats remains a critical bottleneck in Document AI, as traditional multi-stage pipelines suffer from error propagation and limited adaptability to diverse layouts. We introduce layoutRL, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that trains models to be explicitly layout-aware by optimizing a composite reward of normalized edit distance, paragraph count accuracy, and reading order preservation. Leveraging our newly released dataset, Infinity-Doc-55K, which combines 55K high-fidelity synthetic scanned document parsing data with expert-filtered real-world documents, we instantiate layoutRL in a vision-language-model-based parser called Infinity-Parser. Evaluated on English and Chinese benchmarks for OCR, table and formula extraction, and reading order detection, Infinity-Parser achieves new state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and structural fidelity, outpacing specialist pipelines and general-purpose vision-language models. We will publicly release our code and dataset to accelerate progress in robust document understanding.
title: Infinity Parser: Layout Aware Reinforcement Learning for Scanned Document Parsing

abstract: High-quality labeled data is essential for training accurate document conversion models, particularly in domains with complex formats such as tables, formulas, and multi-column text. However, manual annotation is both costly and time-consuming, while automatic labeling using existing models often lacks accuracy in handling such challenging scenarios. Consequently, training student models by distilling outputs from teacher models can significantly limit their performance in real-world applications. In this paper, we propose a fully automated, distillation-free framework comprising two stages for constructing high-quality document extraction datasets and models capable of handling diverse document formats and layouts. In the first stage, we introduce a method for generating large-scale, diverse synthetic data, which enables a model to extract key elements in a unified format with strong initial performance. In the second stage, we present a self-improvement approach that further adapts the model, initially trained on synthetic data, to real-world documents. Specifically, we first use the fine-tuned model to annotate real documents, then apply a suite of filtering strategies to verify annotation quality, and finally retrain the model on the verified dataset. By iteratively repeating this process, we progressively enhance both the model's conversion capabilities and the quality of the generated data. We train a public POINTS-1.5 model to obtain POINTS-Reader, which surpasses many existing public and proprietary models of comparable or larger size. Our model is available at this https URL.
title: POINTS-Reader: Distillation-Free Adaptation of Vision-Language Models for Document Conversion

abstract: Document parsing aims to transform unstructured PDF images into semi-structured data, facilitating the digitization and utilization of information in diverse domains. While vision language models (VLMs) have significantly advanced this task, achieving reliable, high-quality parsing in real-world scenarios remains challenging. Common practice often selects the top-performing model on standard benchmarks. However, these benchmarks may carry dataset-specific biases, leading to inconsistent model rankings and limited correlation with real-world performance. Moreover, benchmark metrics typically provide only overall scores, which can obscure distinct error patterns in output. This raises a key challenge: how can we reliably and comprehensively assess document parsing quality in the wild? We address this problem with DOCR-Inspector, which formalizes document parsing assessment as fine-grained error detection and analysis. Leveraging VLM-as-a-Judge, DOCR-Inspector analyzes a document image and its parsed output, identifies all errors, assigns them to one of 28 predefined types, and produces a comprehensive quality assessment. To enable this capability, we construct DOCRcase-200K for training and propose the Chain-of-Checklist reasoning paradigm to enable the hierarchical structure of parsing quality assessment. For empirical validation, we introduce DOCRcaseBench, a set of 882 real-world document parsing cases with manual annotations. On this benchmark, DOCR-Inspector-7B outperforms commercial models like Gemini 2.5 Pro, as well as leading open-source models. Further experiments demonstrate that its quality assessments provide valuable guidance for parsing results refinement, making DOCR-Inspector both a practical evaluator and a driver for advancing document parsing systems at scale. Model and code are released at: this https URL.

keyInsights: A detailed, hierarchical error taxonomy combined with a structured VLM reasoning approach like Chain-of-Checklist significantly enhances the accuracy and interpretability of fine-grained document parsing evaluation.

Employing an asymmetric reward function during generative reward policy optimization effectively mitigates VLM over-sensitivity to error-free cases while maintaining high recall for actual error detection.
Fine-grained quality assessments from a VLM-as-a-judge system can provide actionable feedback that consistently guides the refinement and improvement of other document parsing models.
originalProblem: Traditional document parsing evaluation relies on coarse-grained overall scores, which mask specific error patterns and hinder targeted model improvements.
Existing benchmarks exhibit bias and lack consistency, limiting their applicability to diverse real-world document parsing scenarios.
Manual, fine-grained analysis of document parsing errors is prohibitively costly and time-consuming for large-scale assessment.
results: DOCR-Inspector-7B achieved F1 scores of 80.21% for text, 62.11% for table, and 73.81% for equation error detection on DOCRcaseBench, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art VLMs (e.g., +47.31% text F1 over Gemini 2.5 Pro Thinking).

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of CoCL reasoning, which raised the Error Type F1 from 57.12% to 71.96%, and the asymmetric reward function, which led to the highest overall performance in identifying errors.

DOCR-Inspector's detailed feedback successfully guided parsing model refinement, demonstrating reduced edit distances for text (e.g., from 9.80 to 8.90 for MonkeyOCR-3B-Pro) and improved Content Description Metrics for equations.
solution: Developed DOCR-Inspector, a VLM-based framework for automated, fine-grained document parsing quality assessment, featuring a comprehensive, hierarchical taxonomy of 28 error types across text, tables, and equations.
Introduced the Chain-of-Checklist (CoCL) reasoning paradigm, which guides VLMs through a structured, hierarchical verification process to improve error detection and interpretability.
Constructed DOCRcase-200K, a large-scale synthetic dataset for training, and DOCRcaseBench, a real-world benchmark with human annotations, for robust empirical validation.
paper_summary.summary: DOCR-Inspector is a VLM-based framework that enables fine-grained and automated evaluation of document parsing outputs by utilizing a 28-type error taxonomy and Chain-of-Checklist reasoning. It achieved F1 scores of 80.21% for text, 62.11% for table, and 73.81% for equation error detection on the DOCRcaseBench, demonstrating superior performance over existing VLMs.

title: DOCR-Inspector: Fine-Grained and Automated Evaluation of Document Parsing with VLM

abstract: Document Layout Parsing serves as a critical gateway for Artificial Intelligence (AI) to access and interpret the world's vast stores of structured knowledge. This process,which encompasses layout detection, text recognition, and relational understanding, is particularly crucial for empowering next-generation Vision-Language Models. Current methods, however, rely on fragmented, multi-stage pipelines that suffer from error propagation and fail to leverage the synergies of joint training. In this paper, we introduce dots_ocr, a single Vision-Language Model that, for the first time, demonstrates the advantages of jointly learning three core tasks within a unified, end-to-end framework. This is made possible by a highly scalable data engine that synthesizes a vast multilingual corpus, empowering the model to deliver robust performance across a wide array of tasks, encompassing diverse languages, layouts, and domains. The efficacy of our unified paradigm is validated by state-of-the-art performance on the comprehensive OmniDocBench. Furthermore, to catalyze research in global document intelligence, we introduce XDocParse, a challenging new benchmark spanning 126 languages. On this benchmark, dots_ocr achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering an approximately 10% relative improvement and demonstrating strong multilingual capability.

keyInsights: Jointly learning detection, recognition, and relational understanding in a unified VLM architecture effectively mitigates error propagation and harnesses synergistic benefits across tasks.
Text recognition acts as a semantic regularizer, guiding the detector towards more legible text regions and improving overall end-to-end parsing performance.
The learning of reading order is deeply intertwined with fundamental visual perception, demonstrating that an incoherent sequence corrupts the model's internal representation of layout.
A meticulously engineered data engine, including targeted error correction, is critical for achieving high-fidelity localization and robust multilingual performance across diverse document types.
originalProblem: Existing document layout parsing (DLP) relies on fragmented, multi-stage pipelines that suffer from error propagation and lack synergy between interdependent tasks.
General Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often lack the architectural priors for precise, fine-grained DLP tasks and are too computationally expensive for large-scale document processing.
A severe scarcity and Anglo-centric bias in current document parsing datasets and evaluation benchmarks limit the development of robust multilingual solutions.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art OverallEdit scores of 0.125 in English and 0.160 in Chinese on the OmniDocBench, outperforming existing specialized expert models and generalist VLMs.

Established a new state-of-the-art on the XDocParse benchmark across 126 languages, demonstrating an Overall Edit distance of 0.177, representing a 29.5% relative error reduction compared to Gemini-2.5-Pro.
Showcased superior component-level performance, including TextEdit scores of 0.032 (EN) and 0.066 (ZH), and state-of-the-art Reading Order Edit scores of 0.040 (EN) and 0.067 (ZH).
solution: Formulated multilingual document parsing as a single, end-to-end autoregressive generation task, unifying layout detection, content recognition, and relational understanding within a single VLM.

Developed a specialized ViT-LLM architecture, featuring a 1.2-billion-parameter Vision Encoder trained from scratch for high-resolution document processing and a customized 1.7-billion-parameter Language Model Decoder.

Introduced a novel three-stage Data Engine that bootstraps multilingual synthesis with a teacher VLM, curates a diverse pre-training corpus by over-sampling under-represented data, and refines the model through human-in-the-loop error correction.
paper_summary.summary: The `dots.ocr` system presents a unified Vision-Language Model for end-to-end multilingual document layout parsing, integrating layout detection, content recognition, and relational understanding. This model achieves state-of-the-art results on the OmniDocBench and records a 29.5% relative error reduction on the XDocParse benchmark, which covers 126 languages, leveraging a custom-built, scalable data synthesis engine.

title: dots.ocr: Multilingual Document Layout Parsing in a Single Vision-Language Model

abstract:
Document parsing is essential for analyzing complex document structures and
extracting fine-grained information, supporting numerous downstream
applications. However, existing methods often require integrating multiple
independent models to handle various parsing tasks, leading to high complexity
and maintenance overhead. To address this, we propose DocFusion, a lightweight
generative model with only 0.28B parameters. It unifies task representations
and achieves collaborative training through an improved objective function.
Experiments reveal and leverage the mutually beneficial interaction among
recognition tasks, and integrating recognition data significantly enhances
detection performance. The final results demonstrate that DocFusion achieves
state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across four key tasks.
title: DocFusion: A Unified Framework for Document Parsing Tasks

abstract: Recently, visually-situated text parsing (VsTP) has experienced notable advancements, driven by the increasing demand for automated document understanding and the emergence of Generative Large Language Models (LLMs) capable of processing document-based questions. Various methods have been proposed to address the challenging problem of VsTP. However, due to the diversified targets and heterogeneous schemas, previous works usually design task-specific architectures and objectives for individual tasks, which inadvertently leads to modal isolation and complex workflow. In this paper, we propose a unified paradigm for parsing visually-situated text across diverse scenarios. Specifically, we devise a universal model, called OmniParser, which can simultaneously handle three typical visually-situated text parsing tasks: text spotting, key information extraction, and table recognition. In OmniParser, all tasks share the unified encoder-decoder architecture, the unified objective: point-conditioned text generation, and the unified input & output representation: prompt & structured sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed OmniParser achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) or highly competitive performances on 7 datasets for the three visually-situated text parsing tasks, despite its unified, concise design. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The two-stage generation strategy effectively decouples the prediction of structural information (via points and tags) from detailed region and content generation, simplifying the learning task and improving interpretability.

Standardizing diverse VsTP tasks into a unified structured sequence generation format allows a single model to handle complex visual-textual understanding end-to-end, fostering better generalizability.

Novel spatial-window and prefix-window prompting pre-training strategies significantly enhance the model's ability to learn complex structures by improving its perception in both coordinate and character-level semantic spaces.
originalProblem: Visually-situated Text Parsing (VsTP) tasks, such as text spotting, KIE, and table recognition, have traditionally relied on specialist models, leading to task-specific architectures, 'modal isolation', and complex, inefficient multi-task pipelines.

Existing unified VsTP models often depend on external OCR engines for text extraction, lack precise localization capabilities, or fail to offer truly end-to-end solutions for all target tasks simultaneously.
The fragmentation across different VsTP tasks results in cumbersome integration, limits in-depth understanding due to fragmented processing, and increases the complexity of workflow and deployment.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art end-to-end recognition F-scores for text spotting on Total-Text (+1.5% None lexicon) and CTW1500 (+3.2% None lexicon), while remaining highly competitive on ICDAR 2015.

Obtained strong KIE performance with an 84.8% field-level F1 score on CORD and the best TED-based accuracy of 93.6% on SROIE, demonstrating robust generalization despite pre-training only on scene text data.

Consistently outperformed previous end-to-end table recognition methods on PubTabNet (88.83 TEDS, 90.45 S-TEDS) and FinTabNet (89.75 TEDS, 91.55 S-TEDS), achieving results comparable to non-end-to-end approaches without relying on external OCR.
solution: A unified encoder-decoder framework is introduced that processes text spotting, key information extraction (KIE), and end-to-end table recognition within a single model.

A novel two-stage generation strategy is employed: first generating a structured points sequence (for text locations and relationships), then in parallel generating polygon sequences (for precise localization) and content sequences (for text transcription).

All tasks are represented using a standardized input/output format based on prompt tokens and structured sequences, utilizing an image encoder (Swin-B) and independent decoders for points, regions, and content.
paper_summary.summary: OmniParser presents a unified deep learning framework capable of simultaneously performing text spotting, key information extraction, and end-to-end table recognition using a two-stage generation strategy. The framework achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across various benchmarks for these visually-situated text parsing tasks, showcasing improved generalizability and simplified workflow.

title: OmniParser: A Unified Framework for Text Spotting, Key Information Extraction and Table Recognition

abstract: Harnessing the full potential of visually-rich documents requires retrieval systems that understand not just text, but intricate layouts, a core challenge in Visual Document Retrieval (VDR). The prevailing multi-vector architectures, while powerful, face a crucial storage bottleneck that current optimization strategies, such as embedding merging, pruning, or using abstract tokens, fail to resolve without compromising performance or ignoring vital layout cues. To address this, we introduce ColParse, a novel paradigm that leverages a document parsing model to generate a small set of layout-informed sub-image embeddings, which are then fused with a global page-level vector to create a compact and structurally-aware multi-vector representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method reduces storage requirements by over 95% while simultaneously yielding significant performance gains across numerous benchmarks and base models. ColParse thus bridges the critical gap between the fine-grained accuracy of multi-vector retrieval and the practical demands of large-scale deployment, offering a new path towards efficient and interpretable multimodal information systems.

keyInsights: Leveraging document parsing effectively disentangles information, allowing for the creation of compact, semantically concentrated multi-vector representations that align with a "Semantic Concentration Axiom".

A dual-stream encoding and global-local fusion strategy captures both fine-grained, layout-specific details and holistic page-level context, providing "Contextual Information Gain" for more robust representations.

The methodology's efficiency stems from replacing hundreds of grid-based vectors with a few layout-informed vectors (typically <10 per page), significantly reducing storage without compromising retrieval quality.
originalProblem: Traditional multi-vector Visual Document Retrieval (VDR) models suffer from prohibitive storage overhead due to numerous patch-level embeddings per page.
Existing optimization strategies for VDR storage reduction often compromise fine-grained information, degrade performance under aggressive compression, or lack explicit grounding in document layout.
The need for layout-aware representations is crucial for effective VDR, as uniform grid-based approaches can lose semantic coherence from intricate document structures.
results: ColParse achieved over 95% storage reduction, storing an average of 5.9 vectors per page while consistently outperforming single-vector baselines and other multi-vector optimization methods.

It demonstrated an average nDCG@5 gain of over 10 points when applied to ten mainstream single-vector retrieval models across 24 VDR datasets, showing robustness as a training-free, plug-and-play module.
The framework enhanced interpretability by pinpointing exact parsed sub-vectors responsible for high relevance scores, enabling fine-grained information back-tracing for specific layout components.
solution: ColParse introduces a plug-and-play framework that uses an off-the-shelf document parsing model to segment document images into a small number of semantically meaningful layout regions (sub-images).
A dual-stream encoder processes both these local sub-images and the entire global document page to generate respective local and global vectors.
These local and global vectors are fused using a weighted element-wise addition to create a compact, structurally-aware multi-vector representation for each document page.
paper_summary.summary: A framework for visual document retrieval, ColParse generates compact, layout-informed multi-vector representations by leveraging document parsing. This approach reduces storage requirements by over 95% while consistently improving retrieval performance, showing average nDCG@5 gains of more than 10 points across various VDR benchmarks and base models.

title: Beyond the Grid: Layout-Informed Multi-Vector Retrieval with Parsed Visual Document Representations

abstract:
Visual Document Understanding has become essential with the increase of
text-rich visual content. This field poses significant challenges due to the
need for effective integration of visual perception and textual comprehension,
particularly across diverse document types with complex layouts. Moreover,
existing fine-tuning datasets for this domain often fall short in providing the
detailed contextual information for robust understanding, leading to
hallucinations and limited comprehension of spatial relationships among visual
elements. To address these challenges, we propose an innovative pipeline that
utilizes adaptive generation of markup languages, such as Markdown, JSON, HTML,
and TiKZ, to build highly structured document representations and deliver
contextually-grounded responses. We introduce two fine-grained structured
datasets: DocMark-Pile, comprising approximately 3.8M pretraining data pairs
for document parsing, and DocMark-Instruct, featuring 624k fine-tuning data
annotations for grounded instruction following. Extensive experiments
demonstrate that our proposed model significantly outperforms existing
state-of-theart MLLMs across a range of visual document understanding
benchmarks, facilitating advanced reasoning and comprehension capabilities in
complex visual scenarios. Our code and models are released at this https URL
com/Euphoria16/DocMark.

organization_info.name: Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, CAS

keyInsights: Using structured markup languages helps capture document semantics and layout
Adaptive generation of suitable markup format improves contextual understanding
Chain-of-thought reasoning through markup intermediates reduces hallucination
originalProblem: Existing MLLMs struggle with complex document formats and layout interpretation
Current visual document understanding datasets lack detailed contextual information and reasoning steps
Models often produce hallucinations and have poor understanding of spatial relationships
results: Outperforms specialized models on text recognition, math expression parsing, and chart extraction
Superior performance on downstream tasks like TextVQA, DocVQA, and MathVision benchmarks
Ablation studies confirm importance of both pretraining and fine-tuning stages
solution: Two-stage training approach combining markup language pretraining and adaptive generation fine-tuning
DocMark-Pile dataset with 3.8M paired examples across 6 markup languages (Markdown, LaTeX, HTML, etc.)
DocMark-Instruct dataset with 624k chain-of-thought reasoning annotations
paper_summary.summary: A visual document understanding framework from CUHK MMLab and vivo AI Lab enables multimodal large language models to process complex documents through adaptive markup language generation, demonstrating superior performance across specialized tasks like text recognition and chart extraction while maintaining contextually-grounded responses through a two-stage training approach with 3.8M markup examples.

title: Adaptive Markup Language Generation for Contextually-Grounded Visual  Document Understanding

abstract: Many documents, that we call templatized documents, are programmatically generated by populating fields in a visual template. Effective data extraction from these documents is crucial to supporting downstream analytical tasks. Current data extraction tools often struggle with complex document layouts, incur high latency and/or cost on large datasets, and often require significant human effort, when extracting tables or values given user-specified fields from documents. The key insight of our tool, TWIX, is to predict the underlying template used to create such documents, modeling the visual and structural commonalities across documents. Data extraction based on this predicted template provides a more principled, accurate, and efficient solution at a low cost. Comprehensive evaluations on 34 diverse real-world datasets show that uncovering the template is crucial for data extraction from templatized documents. TWIX achieves over 90% precision and recall on average, outperforming tools from industry: Textract and Azure Document Intelligence, and vision-based LLMs like GPT-4-Vision, by over 25% in precision and recall. TWIX scales easily to large datasets and is 734X faster and 5836X cheaper than vision-based LLMs for extracting data from a large document collection with 817 pages.

keyInsights: Leveraging shared structural characteristics across documents generated from the same template, where fields appear in consistent positions while values vary, is key to automated extraction.
Precise location vectors and match-based heuristics (perfect, partial matches) can robustly distinguish fixed fields from variable values in document collections.

Formulating template assembly as an optimization problem allows for efficient grouping of functional blocks (tables, key-value pairs) and inference of their hierarchical relationships within a document.
