originalProblem: Extracting structured data from semi-structured, templatized documents (e.g., PDFs) is challenging due to the loss of explicit structural markup and complex nested layouts.
Existing commercial tools and vision-based Large Language Models often struggle with accuracy for complex layouts, incur high costs, and lack scalability for large document collections.
Automatically identifying which elements are fixed 'fields' versus variable 'values' and their relationships within a document template without extensive manual labeling is difficult.
results: Achieved over 90% precision and recall on average across 34 real-world datasets, outperforming industry-standard tools and vision-based LLMs by more than 25%.
Demonstrated substantial efficiency, processing documents 734 times faster and 5836 times cheaper than vision-based LLMs for large collections.
Maintained consistently high performance and low latency/cost across varying document difficulty levels and as the number of documents increased.
solution: UC Berkeley researchers developed TWIX, a system that automatically reconstructs the underlying template from collections of templatized documents by identifying fields and their relationships.
The system uses location vectors and techniques like perfect matches, partial matches, and LLM enhancement for robust field prediction across documents.

TWIX assembles the template by labeling document rows (Key, Value, Key-Value) and inferring the hierarchical structure of functional blocks (e.g., tables, key-value pairs), which then guides data extraction.
paper_summary.summary: UC Berkeley researchers developed TWIX, a system that automatically reconstructs the underlying templates from collections of templatized documents and uses this knowledge to accurately extract structured data. It achieved over 90% precision and recall on real-world datasets, operating 734 times faster and 5836 times cheaper than vision-based LLMs.

title: TWIX: Automatically Reconstructing Structured Data from Templatized Documents

abstract: With recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), grounding and referring capabilities have gained increasing attention for achieving detailed understanding and flexible user interaction. However, these capabilities still remain underdeveloped in visual document understanding due to the scarcity of fine-grained datasets and comprehensive benchmarks. To fill this gap, we propose the DOcument Grounding and Referring data engine (DOGR-Engine), which generates two types of high-quality fine-grained document data: (1) multi-granular parsing data to improve text localization and recognition, and (2) instruction-tuning data to activate MLLMs' grounding and referring capabilities in dialogue and reasoning. Using the DOGR-Engine, we construct DOGR-Bench, a benchmark covering seven grounding and referring tasks across three document types (chart, poster, and PDF document), offering a comprehensive evaluation of fine-grained document understanding. Leveraging the generated data, we further develop DOGR, a strong baseline model that excels in text localization and recognition, while precisely grounds and refers to key textual information during conversation and reasoning, thereby advancing document understanding to a finer granularity and enable flexible interaction paradigms.

keyInsights: High-quality, multi-granular parsing data, particularly derived from methods like re-rendering and PDF parsing with merge strategies, is crucial for developing foundational text localization and recognition abilities in MLLMs for complex documents.

Instruction-tuning data specifically designed to integrate grounding and referring capabilities within dialogue and reasoning contexts effectively activates these advanced interaction paradigms in MLLMs.

A three-stage training methodology that progressively aligns vision-language features, builds document parsing capabilities, and then fine-tunes for instruction following is effective for achieving versatile document grounding and referring performance.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with fine-grained visual document understanding, including precise text localization and region-level recognition.
A lack of clear task definitions, high-quality fine-grained datasets, and comprehensive benchmarks specifically for document grounding and referring tasks limits MLLM development.

Existing approaches often rely on Optical Character Recognition (OCR) tools which introduce inaccuracies and typically do not integrate grounding and referring into broader dialogue and reasoning processes.
results: DOGR achieved an F1_all score of 86.64% for text localization and 77.88% for text recognition on DocLocal4K, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art MLLMs like GPT-4o (5.27% and 4.38% respectively).

On DOGR-Bench, DOGR demonstrated superior performance in grounding (e.g., 73.0% F1_all for Grounded Answering, 52.5% for Grounded Reasoning) and referring (60.3% accuracy for Referring Text) tasks, showcasing advancements over existing models.

DOGR maintained competitive performance on 10 traditional document understanding benchmarks, outperforming previous state-of-the-art models such as IXC2.5-7B by 5.3% accuracy on WTQ and by 25.0% CIDEr on VisualMRC.
solution: Developed DOGR-Engine, a data generation engine that automatically creates 2.1M multi-granular parsing data (word, phrase, line, paragraph, full-page) and 700K instruction-tuning data using re-rendering and post-annotating strategies.

Established DOGR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark with seven distinct tasks across three diverse document types (charts, posters, PDF documents) to systematically evaluate MLLMs' grounding and referring capabilities.

Created DOGR, a multimodal LLM based on InternViT-300M and Qwen2-7B-Instruct, trained with a three-stage strategy (pre-aligning, pre-training with parsing data, fine-tuning with instruction data) to enable fine-grained document understanding and interaction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Xi'an Jiaotong University and ARC Lab (Tencent PCG) introduced DOGR, a multimodal large language model for visual document grounding and referring, supported by a data generation engine (DOGR-Engine) and a benchmark (DOGR-Bench). DOGR achieves strong performance in text localization and recognition, with an F1_all score of 86.64% on DocLocal4K, and shows superior capabilities across novel grounding and referring tasks defined in DOGR-Bench.

title: DOGR: Towards Versatile Visual Document Grounding and Referring

abstract: Automating the annotation of scanned documents is challenging, requiring a balance between computational efficiency and accuracy. DocParseNet addresses this by combining deep learning and multi-modal learning to process both text and visual data. This model goes beyond traditional OCR and semantic segmentation, capturing the interplay between text and images to preserve contextual nuances in complex document structures. Our evaluations show that DocParseNet significantly outperforms conventional models, achieving mIoU scores of 49.12 on validation and 49.78 on the test set. This reflects a 58% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art baseline models and an 18% gain compared to the UNext baseline. Remarkably, DocParseNet achieves these results with only 2.8 million parameters, reducing the model size by approximately 25 times and speeding up training by 5 times compared to other models. These metrics, coupled with a computational efficiency of 0.039 TFLOPs (BS=1), highlight DocParseNet's high performance in document annotation. The model's adaptability and scalability make it well-suited for real-world corporate document processing applications. The code is available at this https URL
title: DocParseNet: Advanced Semantic Segmentation and OCR Embeddings for Efficient Scanned Document Annotation

abstract: Document AI has advanced rapidly and is attracting increasing attention. Yet, while most efforts have focused on document layout analysis (DLA), its generative counterpart, layout generation, remains underexplored. Distinct from traditional graphic layout design and room layout planning, document layout generation typically involves a larger number of elements per page and exhibits greater structural diversity and complexity. Currently, a major obstacle lies in the scarcity of diverse document layouts: academic papers with Manhattan-style structures dominate existing studies, while open-world genres such as newspapers and magazines remain severely underrepresented. To address this gap, we curate OmniDocLayout-1M, the first million-scale dataset of diverse document layouts, covering six common document types and comprising contemporary layouts collected from multiple sources. Moreover, since existing methods struggle in complex domains and often fail to arrange long sequences coherently, we introduce OmniDocLayout-LLM, a 0.5B model with designed two-stage Coarse-to-Fine learning paradigm:1) learning universal layout principles from our dataset with coarse category definitions, and 2) transferring the knowledge to a specific domain with few fine-grained annotated samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves strong performance on multiple domains in M$^6$Doc dataset, substantially surpassing both existing layout generation experts and several latest general-purpose LLMs. Our code, dataset, and models will be publicly released.

keyInsights: A large-scale and diverse dataset is crucial for training generalizable document layout generation models that can handle the complexity and variety of real-world documents.

A coarse-to-fine learning strategy allows a relatively lightweight LLM to effectively acquire both fundamental layout aesthetics from broad data and specific, fine-grained adaptations for complex domains with limited annotations.

Domain-specific training with a specialized learning paradigm provides a substantial advantage, enabling smaller LLMs to achieve superior performance compared to much larger general-purpose LLMs in specialized generative tasks.
originalProblem: Existing document layout datasets suffer from scarcity and domain bias, primarily featuring simple academic layouts and limiting model generalization to diverse real-world documents.
Current layout generation methods struggle with complex layouts that involve numerous fine-grained elements, diverse structural rules, and long-sequence dependencies.

LLM-based approaches for layout generation often require extensive prompt engineering or retrieval heuristics, while large domain-agnostic models are computationally expensive and lack specialized optimization for this task.
results: OmniDocLayout-1M dataset was validated by human studies, demonstrating over 92% perceived quality similarity to human annotations, confirming its high utility for training.

OmniDocLayout-LLM achieved state-of-the-art performance on the M6Doc dataset, consistently outperforming diffusion-based and other LLM-based models across five document types and five generation tasks in metrics like mIoU and FID.

The 0.5B OmniDocLayout-LLM significantly surpassed powerful general-purpose LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Claude-3.7-Sonnet) in zero-shot and few-shot settings, showcasing its superior domain-specific capabilities and efficiency.
Ablation studies confirmed the high effectiveness of the coarse-to-fine learning paradigm, where the combined approach consistently yielded superior performance compared to either stage alone.
solution: Curated OmniDocLayout-1M, a million-scale dataset with approximately 1 million samples and 48 million element instances, collected from 36 diverse sources and covering six contemporary document types through automated and fine-tuned annotation.
Developed OmniDocLayout-LLM, a 0.5B language model that formulates layout generation as conditional sequence modeling, using a pure string-based, prefix-aware representation for layout elements.

Introduced a novel two-stage Coarse-to-Fine learning paradigm: first, training on OmniDocLayout-1M with coarse-grained labels to learn universal layout principles, and then fine-tuning on specific complex domains with fine-grained labels for efficient adaptation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and Sun Yat-sen University developed OmniDocLayout-1M, the first million-scale dataset of diverse document layouts, and OmniDocLayout-LLM, a 0.5B model employing a coarse-to-fine learning paradigm. This approach enabled the model to achieve state-of-the-art performance in generating various document layouts, consistently surpassing larger general-purpose LLMs and existing specialized models.

title: OmniDocLayout: Towards Diverse Document Layout Generation via Coarse-to-Fine LLM Learning

abstract: Information Extraction from visually rich documents is a challenging task that has gained a lot of attention in recent years due to its importance in several document-control based applications and its widespread commercial value. The majority of the research work conducted on this topic to date follow a two-step pipeline. First, they read the text using an off-the-shelf Optical Character Recognition (OCR) engine, then, they extract the fields of interest from the obtained text. The main drawback of these approaches is their dependence on an external OCR system, which can negatively impact both performance and computational speed. Recent OCR-free methods were proposed to address the previous issues. Inspired by their promising results, we propose in this paper an OCR-free end-to-end information extraction model named DocParser. It differs from prior end-to-end approaches by its ability to better extract discriminative character features. DocParser achieves state-of-the-art results on various datasets, while still being faster than previous works.
title: DocParser: End-to-end OCR-free Information Extraction from Visually Rich Documents

abstract:
Scaling architectures have been proven effective for improving Scene Text
Recognition (STR), but the individual contribution of vision encoder and text
decoder scaling remain under-explored. In this work, we present an in-depth
empirical analysis and demonstrate that, contrary to previous observations,
scaling the decoder yields significant performance gains, always exceeding
those achieved by encoder scaling alone. We also identify label noise as a key
challenge in STR, particularly in real-world data, which can limit the
effectiveness of STR models. To address this, we propose Cloze
Self-Distillation (CSD), a method that mitigates label noise by distilling a
student model from context-aware soft predictions and pseudolabels generated by
a teacher model. Additionally, we enhance the decoder architecture by
introducing differential cross-attention for STR. Our methodology achieves
state-of-the-art performance on 10 out of 11 benchmarks using only real data,
while significantly reducing the parameter size and computational costs.

keyInsights: Decoder scaling is crucial for achieving optimal STR performance and proves to be more parameter and computationally efficient than solely scaling the encoder.
Cloze Self-Distillation effectively mitigates the adverse effects of label noise in real-world training data, consistently improving model robustness and accuracy across various data scales.

Architectural enhancements like Differential Cross-Attention allow the decoder to more precisely discriminate between relevant and noisy visual and contextual features, yielding consistent performance gains.
originalProblem: Prior STR scaling analyses suggested that increasing decoder depth leads to performance degradation, directing research focus primarily to encoder scaling.
Real-world training datasets for STR frequently contain significant label noise, which limits the accuracy and generalization capabilities of models.
Existing decoder architectures in STR models possess limitations in processing contextual information effectively and suppressing irrelevant noise.
results: The CSD-D model achieved state-of-the-art performance on 10 out of 11 benchmarks, outperforming previous SOTA (e.g., CLIP4STR-L) using only 24.7% of its parameters and 23.9% of its GFLOPs.
Decoder-focused scaling configurations, such as ViT-B paired with PLD-L (114.6M parameters), achieved 92.80% AVG11, surpassing CLIP4STR-H (1 Billion parameters) in efficiency.
CSD consistently provided approximately 0.5% AVG11 accuracy gain on both the full Real and RBU datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving performance on noisy data.
solution: Performed an extensive empirical analysis of independent encoder and decoder scaling, demonstrating that decoder scaling is effective and efficient.
Introduced Cloze Self-Distillation (CSD), a three-step process where a teacher model generates context-aware pseudolabels and soft predictions to distill a student model, counteracting label noise.
Developed a Differential Permutation Language Decoder (PLD-Diff) by incorporating Differential Cross-Attention and SwiGLU activations to improve contextual focus and noise reduction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Samsung R&D Institute UK and Samsung Electronics present an improved Scene Text Recognition (STR) framework that re-evaluates optimal model scaling strategies, integrates Cloze Self-Distillation to mitigate label noise, and enhances the decoder with Differential Cross-Attention. This methodology achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on multiple benchmarks using significantly fewer parameters and computational resources compared to prior approaches.

title: Accurate Scene Text Recognition with Efficient Model Scaling and Cloze  Self-Distillation

abstract: Recently, significant progress has been made applying machine learning to the problem of table structure inference and extraction from unstructured documents. However, one of the greatest challenges remains the creation of datasets with complete, unambiguous ground truth at scale. To address this, we develop a new, more comprehensive dataset for table extraction, called PubTables-1M. PubTables-1M contains nearly one million tables from scientific articles, supports multiple input modalities, and contains detailed header and location information for table structures, making it useful for a wide variety of modeling approaches. It also addresses a significant source of ground truth inconsistency observed in prior datasets called oversegmentation, using a novel canonicalization procedure. We demonstrate that these improvements lead to a significant increase in training performance and a more reliable estimate of model performance at evaluation for table structure recognition. Further, we show that transformer-based object detection models trained on PubTables-1M produce excellent results for all three tasks of detection, structure recognition, and functional analysis without the need for any special customization for these tasks. Data and code will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Large-scale, high-quality, and consistently annotated datasets are crucial for training robust deep learning models that can generalize effectively across diverse table structures.
Canonicalizing ground truth data by resolving ambiguities like oversegmentation significantly improves model performance and provides a more reliable basis for evaluation.

General-purpose deep learning architectures, such as the Detection Transformer (DETR), can achieve state-of-the-art performance across all table extraction subtasks without extensive task-specific customizations, given sufficiently rich and clean data.
originalProblem: Existing deep learning datasets for table extraction were often too small, lacked comprehensive annotations (e.g., for blank cells or row headers), or suffered from inconsistent ground truth.
A major issue in crowd-sourced datasets was "oversegmentation" where logical spanning cells were incorrectly split, creating ambiguous labels that hindered model training and evaluation.
Many state-of-the-art table extraction models required custom pipelines, specialized architectures, or rule-based post-processing, limiting their generality.
results: PubTables-1M provides 947,642 tables with comprehensive annotations, and its canonicalization procedure reduced oversegmented projected row headers to 0%, compared to up to 98.87% in prior datasets.

The Detection Transformer (DETR) model trained on PubTables-1M achieved high performance, with an AP of 0.966 for table detection and an AP of 0.912 for combined table structure recognition and functional analysis.

Models trained on canonicalized data significantly outperformed those trained on non-canonical data, particularly for complex tables (e.g., Acc_Cont 0.6944 vs. 0.2712), demonstrating the direct benefit of consistent ground truth.
solution: PubTables-1M was created by aligning PDF visual content with XML semantic descriptions from the PubMed Central Open Access corpus, then inferring and completing all missing spatial annotations for tables, rows, columns, and grid cells.
A novel canonicalization procedure was developed to correct oversegmentation, ensuring a unique and unambiguous logical structure for each table, particularly for projected row headers.

Automated quality control steps were implemented to filter out errors and ensure ground truth consistency, and an off-the-shelf Detection Transformer (DETR) model was adapted to solve all table extraction tasks as a unified object detection problem.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft researchers introduce PubTables-1M, a dataset featuring nearly one million tables with comprehensive, canonicalized annotations that resolve common structural ambiguities. This dataset enables generic Detection Transformer models to achieve high accuracy across all table extraction subtasks, including table detection, structure recognition, and functional analysis.

title: PubTables-1M: Towards comprehensive table extraction from unstructured documents

abstract: Information extraction from copy-heavy documents, characterized by massive volumes of structurally similar content, represents a critical yet understudied challenge in enterprise document processing. We present a systematic framework that strategically combines OCR engines with Large Language Models (LLMs) to optimize the accuracy-efficiency trade-off inherent in repetitive document extraction tasks. Unlike existing approaches that pursue universal solutions, our method exploits document-specific characteristics through intelligent strategy selection. We implement and evaluate 25 configurations across three extraction paradigms (direct, replacement, and table-based) on identity documents spanning four formats (PNG, DOCX, XLSX, PDF). Through table-based extraction methods, our adaptive framework delivers outstanding results: F1=1.0 accuracy with 0.97s latency for structured documents, and F1=0.997 accuracy with 0.6 s for challenging image inputs when integrated with PaddleOCR, all while maintaining sub-second processing speeds. The 54 times performance improvement compared with multimodal methods over naive approaches, coupled with format-aware routing, enables processing of heterogeneous document streams at production scale. Beyond the specific application to identity extraction, this work establishes a general principle: the repetitive nature of copy-heavy tasks can be transformed from a computational burden into an optimization opportunity through structure-aware method selection.

keyInsights: The optimal information extraction strategy is highly dependent on a document's modality and structural characteristics, challenging the effectiveness of universal, 'one-size-fits-all' models.

The inherently repetitive nature of copy-heavy tasks can be strategically leveraged as an optimization opportunity by exploiting document structure, rather than being treated as a computational burden.
The fidelity of spatial structure preservation by upstream OCR and document parsing tools is more crucial than mere character recognition accuracy for successful and efficient information extraction.
originalProblem: Existing large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive, slow, and prone to hallucination for high-volume, repetitive document processing tasks.
Traditional rule-based and template-specific information extraction systems are brittle, difficult to maintain, and do not scale well with minor document variations.
Most academic benchmarks for document intelligence primarily focus on semantic diversity rather than the unique demands of high-throughput, structurally redundant documents.
results: Table-based extraction methods achieved near-perfect F1 scores (e.g., 0.997 to 1.000) with sub-second latencies (0.3 to 1.6 seconds) when document structural information was effectively preserved.

For image-based documents, the PaddleOCR + Table-based extraction method delivered near-perfect accuracy (F1 = 0.997) with a 54x speedup (0.63 seconds/document) compared to direct multimodal LLM processing (33.9 seconds/document).

For structured office documents (DOCX/XLSX), table-based methods achieved perfect F1 scores (1.000) with up to 44x acceleration over direct LLM extraction, capitalizing on inherent tabular structures.
solution: A modular, hybrid framework that intelligently combines specialized OCR and parsing tools with LLM-based extraction strategies (Direct, Replacement-based, Table-based).
Dynamic selection of the most appropriate extraction method is performed based on the document's modality (image, DOCX, XLSX, PDF) and structural characteristics.
Utilizes Qwen2.5-7B for text processing and Qwen2.5-VL-7B for vision-language tasks, chosen for their balance of performance and efficiency at the 7B scale.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Ningbo Institute of Digital Twin, Eastern Institute of Technology, developed a hybrid OCR-LLM framework to efficiently extract information from enterprise-scale copy-heavy documents. This framework achieved sub-second latency and near-perfect F1 scores across various document types, demonstrating up to a 54x speedup over multimodal approaches by leveraging document structure.

title: Hybrid OCR-LLM Framework for Enterprise-Scale Document Information Extraction Under Copy-heavy Task

abstract: While concept-based interpretability methods have traditionally focused on local explanations of neural network predictions, we propose a novel framework and interactive tool that extends these methods into the domain of mechanistic interpretability. Our approach enables a global dissection of model behavior by analyzing how high-level semantic attributes (referred to as concepts) emerge, interact, and propagate through internal model components. Unlike prior work that isolates individual neurons or predictions, our framework systematically quantifies how semantic concepts are represented across layers, revealing latent circuits and information flow that underlie model decision-making. A key innovation is our visualization platform that we named BAGEL (for Bias Analysis with a Graph for global Explanation Layers), which presents these insights in a structured knowledge graph, allowing users to explore concept-class relationships, identify spurious correlations, and enhance model trustworthiness. Our framework is model-agnostic, scalable, and contributes to a deeper understanding of how deep learning models generalize (or fail to) in the presence of dataset biases. The demonstration is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: High-level semantic concepts demonstrate a clear progression and refinement across network layers, with early layers processing low-level features and deeper layers emphasizing complex object parts.
The framework effectively distinguishes between dataset-specific biases that the model did not learn and model-specific spurious correlations not present in the original dataset.

By uniformly analyzing all layers through Global Average Pooling, BAGEL provides a global perspective on concept alignment, enabling a scalable approach to diagnose model vulnerabilities beyond localized explanations.
originalProblem: Deep neural networks operate as black boxes, hindering trust and accountability in safety-critical applications.
Existing XAI methods often provide only local explanations, failing to uncover global patterns of bias or spurious correlations within models.
Current concept-based interpretability methods frequently rely on costly manual annotations or limited concept sets, restricting their scalability and comprehensive analysis across all model layers.
results: Qualitative analysis with the interactive Knowledge Graph successfully visualizes complex concept-class relationships and biases, identifying instances like DenseNet121 overfitting to environmental contexts for animal classification.

BAGEL consistently achieved strong quantitative recall in detecting dataset biases, with BAGEL F1 scoring up to 0.875 for 'Husky vs. Wolf' and 0.800 for 'Derm7pt', outperforming baseline methods like TCAV and SAE.

The framework revealed the hierarchical emergence of conceptual categories across layers, with 'materials' and 'textures' prominent in initial layers and 'object parts' gaining importance in deeper layers.
solution: BAGEL introduces a model-agnostic framework that systematically quantifies high-level semantic concept representation across every internal layer of a deep neural network.
It uses zero-shot concept detection with CLIP to quantify dataset biases and trains simple logistic regression classifiers on layer embeddings to estimate model-learned concept prevalence.

The framework compares dataset and model biases using Weighted F1-Score and Jensen-Shannon Divergence, visualizing these relationships and discrepancies via an interactive, color-coded knowledge graph.
paper_summary.summary: A new framework, BAGEL, developed by researchers at U2IS, ENSTA, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, and EPFL, offers global, concept-based mechanistic interpretability for deep neural networks. It systematically quantifies how high-level semantic concepts are represented across all internal layers and compares dataset biases with model-learned biases, enhancing understanding of model generalization and trustworthiness.

title: Concept-Based Mechanistic Interpretability Using Structured Knowledge Graphs

abstract: We introduce MinerU2.5, a 1.2B-parameter document parsing vision-language model that achieves state-of-the-art recognition accuracy while maintaining exceptional computational efficiency. Our approach employs a coarse-to-fine, two-stage parsing strategy that decouples global layout analysis from local content recognition. In the first stage, the model performs efficient layout analysis on downsampled images to identify structural elements, circumventing the computational overhead of processing high-resolution inputs. In the second stage, guided by the global layout, it performs targeted content recognition on native-resolution crops extracted from the original image, preserving fine-grained details in dense text, complex formulas, and tables. To support this strategy, we developed a comprehensive data engine that generates diverse, large-scale training corpora for both pretraining and fine-tuning. Ultimately, MinerU2.5 demonstrates strong document parsing ability, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, surpassing both general-purpose and domain-specific models across various recognition tasks, while maintaining significantly lower computational overhead.
title: MinerU2.5: A Decoupled Vision-Language Model for Efficient High-Resolution Document Parsing

abstract: The advent of "OCR 2.0" and large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has set new benchmarks in text recognition. However, these unified architectures often come with significant computational demands, challenges in precise text localization within complex layouts, and a propensity for textual hallucinations. Revisiting the prevailing notion that model scale is the sole path to high accuracy, this paper introduces PP-OCRv5, a meticulously optimized, lightweight OCR system with merely 5 million parameters. We demonstrate that PP-OCRv5 achieves performance competitive with many billion-parameter VLMs on standard OCR benchmarks, while offering superior localization precision and reduced hallucinations. The cornerstone of our success lies not in architectural expansion but in a data-centric investigation. We systematically dissect the role of training data by quantifying three critical dimensions: data difficulty, data accuracy, and data diversity. Our extensive experiments reveal that with a sufficient volume of high-quality, accurately labeled, and diverse data, the performance ceiling for traditional, efficient two-stage OCR pipelines is far higher than commonly assumed. This work provides compelling evidence for the viability of lightweight, specialized models in the large-model era and offers practical insights into data curation for OCR. The source code and models are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Optimal model performance for lightweight OCR models is achieved by training on a 'sweet spot' of data difficulty (samples with 0.95-0.97 confidence scores), which balances challenge and reliable labeling.

The model demonstrates resilience to label noise, with a 20% label corruption rate leading to only a 1.33 percentage point decrease in recognition accuracy, suggesting robustness for leveraging large, automatically annotated datasets.

Increasing training data diversity, as measured by feature clusters, significantly improves model generalization, resulting in a 5.38 percentage point accuracy gain for a fixed data volume (600k samples).
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often exhibit imprecise text localization, textual hallucinations, and high computational inefficiency when applied to specialized OCR tasks.
Traditional lightweight OCR models have often reached a performance plateau, primarily focusing on architectural innovations rather than systematic data quality improvements.
There is a need for OCR solutions that combine high accuracy with practical deployability, particularly in resource-constrained industrial settings.
results: PP-OCRv5 achieved an 80.1% weighted average accuracy on an in-house multi-scenario benchmark, showing a substantial improvement over previous versions like PP-OCRv4 (53.0%).

On the OmniDocBench public benchmark, PP-OCRv5 achieved an average edit distance of 0.067, performing competitively with or superior to many specialized OCR tools and demonstrating robustness in challenging categories where larger VLMs showed degradation.

The 5M-parameter PP-OCRv5 demonstrated that a data-centric approach can enable lightweight models to rival billion-parameter VLMs in core OCR tasks, offering superior localization, reduced hallucinations, and significantly lower computational cost.
solution: PP-OCRv5, a two-stage OCR system with approximately 5 million parameters, was developed, utilizing a DB algorithm for text detection and a SVTR_LCNet architecture with Guided Training of CTC (GTC) for recognition.
A systematic data-centric approach was employed to optimize the recognition model's training data, focusing on quantitative analysis of data difficulty, accuracy (label noise), and diversity.
A 22.6 million-sample, multi-source, multi-scenario training dataset was constructed based on empirically validated data curation principles to broaden the model's application scope.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Baidu Inc. developed PP-OCRv5, a 5-million-parameter optical character recognition (OCR) system that achieves performance competitive with billion-parameter Vision-Language Models on specialized OCR tasks. This was accomplished through a systematic data-centric approach, optimizing training data for difficulty, accuracy, and diversity, leading to a highly accurate and computationally efficient solution.

title: PP-OCRv5: A Specialized 5M-Parameter Model Rivaling Billion-Parameter Vision-Language Models on OCR Tasks

abstract: The advancement of Document Intelligence (DI) demands large-scale, high-quality training data, yet manual annotation remains a critical bottleneck. While data generation methods are evolving rapidly, existing surveys are constrained by fragmented focuses on single modalities or specific tasks, lacking a unified perspective aligned with real-world workflows. To fill this gap, this survey establishes the first comprehensive technical map for data generation in DI. Data generation is redefined as supervisory signal production, and a novel taxonomy is introduced based on the "availability of data and labels." This framework organizes methodologies into four resource-centric paradigms: Data Augmentation, Data Generation from Scratch, Automated Data Annotation, and Self-Supervised Signal Construction. Furthermore, a multi-level evaluation framework is established to integrate intrinsic quality and extrinsic utility, compiling performance gains across diverse DI benchmarks. Guided by this unified structure, the methodological landscape is dissected to reveal critical challenges such as fidelity gaps and frontiers including co-evolutionary ecosystems. Ultimately, by systematizing this fragmented field, data generation is positioned as the central engine for next-generation DI.

keyInsights: The proposed resource-centric taxonomy helps unify fragmented research and provides practical guidance for selecting data generation methods based on specific resource constraints.
A "Perception-Reasoning Shift" is observed, with Data Generation from Scratch dominating perception tasks and Automated Data Annotation (especially LLM-driven) leading for high-level reasoning tasks.

The future of data generation involves addressing fidelity and consistency challenges while exploring frontiers like agentic generation, knowledge injection, and co-evolutionary ecosystems with "Negentropy Filters."
originalProblem: Document Intelligence (DI) advancements require vast quantities of high-quality, multimodal, and fine-grained annotated training data, which is challenging to acquire.
Manual data annotation for DI is prohibitively expensive, time-consuming, and insufficient to meet the scale and diversity demands of modern models.
The rapidly growing field of data generation for DI suffers from fragmentation, lacking a unified understanding and comprehensive overview beyond single modalities or specific tasks.
results: The paper establishes the first comprehensive technical map for data generation in Document Intelligence, redefining its scope to include all non-manual supervisory signal production methods.

A multi-level evaluation framework is introduced, enabling assessment of generated data based on both intrinsic quality (e.g., fidelity, diversity) and extrinsic utility (downstream model performance).

The analysis demonstrates quantitative performance gains, such as a 7.9% WER reduction for text recognition using Data Generation from Scratch, a 9.02% mAP improvement for layout analysis, and up to an 8.11% F1 boost for information extraction through various data generation paradigms.
solution: The authors redefine data generation as "supervisory signal production," encompassing all non-manual methods for creating, augmenting, or automatically generating training signals for Visually-Rich Documents (VRDs).

They propose a novel, resource-centric taxonomy for data generation methods based on data and label availability, classifying approaches into Data Augmentation, Data Generation from Scratch, Automated Data Annotation, and Self-Supervised Signal Construction.

A multi-level evaluation framework is established to assess generated data, integrating both intrinsic quality metrics (e.g., fidelity, diversity) and extrinsic utility measures (downstream model performance).
paper_summary.summary: This survey by Ying et al. provides the first comprehensive technical map and a novel, resource-centric taxonomy for data generation methods in Document Intelligence, redefining data generation as "supervisory signal production." It introduces a multi-level evaluation framework and analyzes the field's shift towards generative AI and self-supervised learning, with specific paradigm prevalence for perception and reasoning tasks across various DI benchmarks.

title: Beyond Human Annotation: Recent Advances in Data Generation Methods for Document Intelligence

abstract: Conventional document layout analysis (DLA) traditionally depends on empirical priors or a fixed set of learnable queries executed in a single forward pass. While sufficient for early-generation documents with a small, predetermined number of regions, this paradigm struggles with contemporary documents, which exhibit diverse element counts and increasingly complex layouts. To address challenges posed by modern documents, we present HybriDLA, a novel generative framework that unifies diffusion and autoregressive decoding within a single layer. The diffusion component iteratively refines bounding-box hypotheses, whereas the autoregressive component injects semantic and contextual awareness, enabling precise region prediction even in highly varied layouts. To further enhance detection quality, we design a multi-scale feature-fusion encoder that captures both fine-grained and high-level visual cues. This architecture elevates performance to 83.5% mean Average Precision (mAP). Extensive experiments on the DocLayNet and M$^6$Doc benchmarks demonstrate that HybriDLA sets a state-of-the-art performance, outperforming previous approaches. All data and models will be made publicly available at this https URL.
title: HybriDLA: Hybrid Generation for Document Layout Analysis

abstract: Visual document understanding (VDU) is a challenging task that involves understanding documents across various modalities (text and image) and layouts (forms, tables, etc.). This study aims to enhance generalizability of small VDU models by distilling knowledge from LLMs. We identify that directly prompting LLMs often fails to generate informative and useful data. In response, we present a new framework (called DocKD) that enriches the data generation process by integrating external document knowledge. Specifically, we provide an LLM with various document elements like key-value pairs, layouts, and descriptions, to elicit open-ended answers. Our experiments show that DocKD produces high-quality document annotations and surpasses the direct knowledge distillation approach that does not leverage external document knowledge. Moreover, student VDU models trained with solely DocKD-generated data are not only comparable to those trained with human-annotated data on in-domain tasks but also significantly excel them on out-of-domain tasks.
title: DocKD: Knowledge Distillation from LLMs for Open-World Document Understanding Models

abstract: Accurate extraction of key information from 2D engineering drawings is crucial for high-precision manufacturing. Manual extraction is slow and labor-intensive, while traditional Optical Character Recognition (OCR) techniques often struggle with complex layouts and overlapping symbols, resulting in unstructured outputs. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel hybrid deep learning framework for structured information extraction by integrating an Oriented Bounding Box (OBB) detection model with a transformer-based document parsing model (Donut). An in-house annotated dataset is used to train YOLOv11 for detecting nine key categories: Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T), General Tolerances, Measures, Materials, Notes, Radii, Surface Roughness, Threads, and Title Blocks. Detected OBBs are cropped into images and labeled to fine-tune Donut for structured JSON output. Fine-tuning strategies include a single model trained across all categories and category-specific models. Results show that the single model consistently outperforms category-specific ones across all evaluation metrics, achieving higher precision (94.77% for GD&T), recall (100% for most categories), and F1 score (97.3%), while reducing hallucinations (5.23%). The proposed framework improves accuracy, reduces manual effort, and supports scalable deployment in precision-driven industries.

organization_info.name: Agency for Science Technology and Research (A*STAR)

keyInsights: Combining robust OBB detection with an OCR-free vision-language transformer provides an effective strategy for structured information extraction from the intricate and context-rich domain of engineering drawings.

A single, generalized Donut model trained across all annotation categories achieves better overall performance, exhibiting higher precision and lower hallucination rates than multiple specialized category-specific models.
Directly generating structured JSON output enables seamless integration with digital manufacturing systems, significantly reducing manual post-processing and facilitating Industry 4.0 initiatives.
originalProblem: Manual extraction of information from 2D engineering drawings is time-consuming, error-prone, and a significant bottleneck in precision manufacturing.

Existing automated tools and generic Optical Character Recognition (OCR) models struggle with complex layouts, non-standard symbols, contextual ambiguity, and produce unstructured output requiring further processing.
Previous domain-specific machine learning efforts, including fine-tuned OCR, showed degraded performance with complex drawing layouts due to challenges in robust segmentation.
results: The single Donut model achieved higher precision across all feature categories, for example, 89.7% for Surface Roughness compared to 65.4% for category-specific models.
The single model also demonstrated significantly lower hallucination rates across most categories, reducing Surface Roughness hallucination from 34.6% (category-specific) to 10.3%.
Both training strategies maintained high recall in most categories, though hallucination rates remained high for categories with scarce training data like Materials and General Tolerances.
solution: A two-stage hybrid deep learning framework integrating YOLOv11 for Oriented Bounding Box (OBB) detection to accurately segment various annotations in engineering drawings.
A fine-tuned Donut (Document Understanding Transformer) model that processes visual input directly to generate structured JSON outputs, bypassing traditional OCR.

An augmented dataset of 1,367 2D engineering drawings, with a subset of 1,000 image patches manually annotated for structured JSON ground truth and expanded to 6,000 through data augmentation to enhance model generalization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from A*STAR and Nanyang Technological University developed a hybrid deep learning framework for automated structured information extraction from 2D engineering drawings. The framework utilizes YOLOv11 for oriented bounding box detection and a fine-tuned Donut transformer, demonstrating superior precision and significantly reduced hallucination rates with a single, generalized model compared to category-specific models.

title: Automated Parsing of Engineering Drawings for Structured Information Extraction Using a Fine-tuned Document Understanding Transformer

abstract: Document parsing has garnered widespread attention as vision-language models (VLMs) advance OCR capabilities. However, the field remains fragmented across dozens of specialized models with varying strengths, forcing users to navigate complex model selection and limiting system scalability. Moreover, existing two-stage approaches depend on axis-aligned bounding boxes for layout detection, failing to handle distorted or photographed documents effectively. To this end, we present Dolphin-v2, a two-stage document image parsing model that substantially improves upon the original Dolphin. In the first stage, Dolphin-v2 jointly performs document type classification (digital-born versus photographed) alongside layout analysis. For digital-born documents, it conducts finer-grained element detection with reading order prediction. In the second stage, we employ a hybrid parsing strategy: photographed documents are parsed holistically as complete pages to handle geometric distortions, while digital-born documents undergo element-wise parallel parsing guided by the detected layout anchors, enabling efficient content extraction. Compared with the original Dolphin, Dolphin-v2 introduces several crucial enhancements: (1) robust parsing of photographed documents via holistic page-level understanding, (2) finer-grained element detection (21 categories) with semantic attribute extraction such as author information and document metadata, and (3) code block recognition with indentation preservation, which existing systems typically lack. Comprehensive evaluations are conducted on DocPTBench, OmniDocBench, and our self-constructed RealDoc-160 benchmark. The results demonstrate substantial improvements: +14.78 points overall on the challenging OmniDocBench and 91% error reduction on photographed documents, while maintaining efficient inference through parallel processing.

keyInsights: Document type classification is crucial; dynamically switching parsing strategies based on document characteristics (e.g., photographed vs. digital) leads to substantial performance gains (e.g., 377% degradation without it).
Employing absolute pixel coordinates for bounding box prediction significantly improves localization precision and prevents information loss in high-resolution documents.
Dedicated parsing modules and specialized prompts are essential for accurately extracting complex content such as LaTeX formulas and preserving the structural integrity of code blocks.
originalProblem: Existing document parsing solutions were fragmented, lacking a universal model to handle both pristine digital and distorted photographed documents.

Previous two-stage parsing approaches, including the original Dolphin, struggled with geometrically distorted documents due to reliance on axis-aligned bounding boxes and fixed resolution representations.

Models often provided limited element category coverage, lacked semantic attribute extraction, and failed to preserve critical structural details like code indentation or precise formula representation.
results: Dolphin-v2 achieved an 'overall' score of 89.78 points on OmniDocBench, marking a 14.78-point improvement over the original Dolphin.
On the RealDoc-160 benchmark for photographed documents, the model achieved an average Edit Distance of 0.0392, demonstrating a 91% error reduction compared to baseline approaches.

The system successfully preserved code indentation structures, a capability where other advanced models like PaddleOCR-VL faltered, and improved formula parsing accuracy by 3.38 points on OmniDocBench.
solution: A two-stage parsing paradigm based on a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-3B model, where Stage 1 classifies document type (digital vs. photographed) and performs layout analysis using native resolution vision transformers.

A hybrid Stage 2 content parsing strategy: holistic parsing for photographed documents to handle distortions, and efficient element-wise parallel parsing for digital documents guided by layout anchors.

Integration of 21 finer-grained element categories, absolute pixel coordinate prediction for bounding boxes, and specialized prompts for content like LaTeX formulas and indentation-preserving code blocks.
paper_summary.summary: Dolphin-v2, built upon a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-3B, introduces a hybrid two-stage paradigm for universal document parsing, dynamically adapting its approach for digital-born and geometrically distorted photographed documents. The system achieves a 14.78-point improvement on OmniDocBench and a 91% error reduction on photographed documents compared to prior baselines, alongside enhanced recognition of specialized content like code and formulas.

title: Dolphin-v2: Universal Document Parsing via Scalable Anchor Prompting

abstract: Benchmark datasets for table structure recognition (TSR) must be carefully processed to ensure they are annotated consistently. However, even if a dataset's annotations are self-consistent, there may be significant inconsistency across datasets, which can harm the performance of models trained and evaluated on them. In this work, we show that aligning these benchmarks$\unicode{x2014}$removing both errors and inconsistency between them$\unicode{x2014}$improves model performance significantly. We demonstrate this through a data-centric approach where we adopt one model architecture, the Table Transformer (TATR), that we hold fixed throughout. Baseline exact match accuracy for TATR evaluated on the ICDAR-2013 benchmark is 65% when trained on PubTables-1M, 42% when trained on FinTabNet, and 69% combined. After reducing annotation mistakes and inter-dataset inconsistency, performance of TATR evaluated on ICDAR-2013 increases substantially to 75% when trained on PubTables-1M, 65% when trained on FinTabNet, and 81% combined. We show through ablations over the modification steps that canonicalization of the table annotations has a significantly positive effect on performance, while other choices balance necessary trade-offs that arise when deciding a benchmark dataset's final composition. Overall we believe our work has significant implications for benchmark design for TSR and potentially other tasks as well. Dataset processing and training code will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Careful alignment and quality control of benchmark datasets are crucial for improving TSR model performance and generalization, independent of model architecture changes.
The canonicalization process, which corrects oversegmentation and infers consistent header structures, has a particularly positive impact on model performance.
Maintaining a clean and consistent test set is essential for accurate model evaluation, as demonstrated by significant performance shifts when the ICDAR-2013 benchmark itself was corrected.
originalProblem: Benchmark datasets for machine learning often contain errors and inconsistencies, which can lead to inflated performance metrics and hinder true progress.

Significant inter-dataset inconsistencies and 'misalignment' exist across different table structure recognition (TSR) benchmarks, degrading model performance when datasets are combined or used for cross-evaluation.
The quality and consistency of training and evaluation data remain a bottleneck, despite advancements in model architectures for TSR.
results: Training on the refined FinTabNet.a6 dataset, compared to the baseline FinTabNet.a1, increased the `Acc_Con` on ICDAR-2013.a3 from 41.1% to 64.6%.

The model trained on a combination of PubTables-1M and the fully aligned FinTabNet.a6 achieved new state-of-the-art baselines of 0.965 DAR and 81% exact match accuracy (`Acc_Con`) on the corrected ICDAR-2013 benchmark.
Improvements to the ICDAR-2013 evaluation set itself (from .a1 to .a3) significantly boosted measured model performance, with the combined model's `Acc_Con` jumping from 68% to 81%.
solution: Adopted a data-centric AI paradigm by fixing the Table Transformer (TATR) model architecture and focusing solely on improving data quality.
Implemented a multi-stage process of identifying, correcting, and aligning annotations in prominent TSR datasets (FinTabNet, ICDAR-2013) through both manual inspection and automated procedures.
Applied a canonicalization procedure to minimize inconsistencies like oversegmentation and infer consistent header structures across datasets, standardizing annotation guidelines.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft researchers demonstrated that systematically aligning and cleaning benchmark datasets for table structure recognition significantly improves model performance and generalization. By applying automated correction and canonicalization to datasets like FinTabNet and ICDAR-2013, a fixed Table Transformer (TATR) model achieved 0.965 DAR and 81% exact match accuracy on the corrected ICDAR-2013 benchmark, validating the impact of a data-centric approach.

title: Aligning benchmark datasets for table structure recognition

abstract: Document parsing, as a fundamental yet crucial vision task, is being revolutionized by vision-language models (VLMs). However, the autoregressive (AR) decoding inherent to VLMs creates a significant bottleneck, severely limiting parsing speed. In this paper, we propose Parallel-Token Prediction (PTP), a plugable, model-agnostic and simple-yet-effective method that enables VLMs to generate multiple future tokens in parallel with improved sample efficiency. Specifically, we insert some learnable tokens into the input sequence and design corresponding training objectives to equip the model with parallel decoding capabilities for document parsing. Furthermore, to support effective training, we develop a comprehensive data generation pipeline that efficiently produces large-scale, high-quality document parsing training data for VLMs. Extensive experiments on OmniDocBench and olmOCR-bench demonstrate that our method not only significantly improves decoding speed (1.6x-2.2x) but also reduces model hallucinations and exhibits strong generalization abilities.
title: Efficient Document Parsing via Parallel Token Prediction

abstract: In this paper, we propose Agentar-Fin-OCR, a document parsing system tailored to financial-domain documents, transforming ultra-long financial PDFs into semantically consistent, highly accurate, structured outputs with auditing-grade provenance. To address finance-specific challenges such as complex layouts, cross-page structural discontinuities, and cell-level referencing capability, Agentar-Fin-OCR combines (1) a Cross-page Contents Consolidation algorithm to restore continuity across pages and a Document-level Heading Hierarchy Reconstruction (DHR) module to build a globally consistent Table of Contents (TOC) tree for structure-aware retrieval, and (2) a difficulty-adaptive curriculum learning training strategy for table parsing, together with a CellBBoxRegressor module that uses structural anchor tokens to localize table cells from decoder hidden states without external detectors. Experiments demonstrate that our model shows high performance on the table parsing metrics of OmniDocBench. To enable realistic evaluation in the financial vertical, we further introduce FinDocBench, a benchmark that includes six financial document categories with expert-verified annotations and evaluation metrics including Table of Contents edit-distance-based similarity (TocEDS), cross-page concatenated TEDS, and Table Cell Intersection over Union (C-IoU). We evaluate a wide range of state-of-the-art models on FinDocBench to assess their capabilities and remaining limitations on financial documents. Overall, Agentar-Fin-OCR and FinDocBench provide a practical foundation for reliable downstream financial document applications.

keyInsights: Document-level processing is essential for maintaining semantic and structural continuity in complex, ultra-long financial documents, effectively overcoming limitations of isolated page-level analysis.

Leveraging visual and spatial cues through a 'pseudo-TOC' significantly improves the accuracy of heading hierarchy reconstruction, particularly for resolving ambiguities in multi-page financial documents.

A curriculum learning strategy combined with reinforcement optimization, guided by difficulty factors like structural complexity, effectively enhances table parsing accuracy for intricate financial layouts and ensures robust cell-level extraction.
originalProblem: Existing document parsing methods primarily focus on page-level analysis, leading to semantic fragmentation and loss of global hierarchical context in long financial documents.

General Vision Language Models (VLMs) frequently struggle with numerical hallucination, small fonts, complex cross-cell dependencies, and maintaining semantic coherence across multiple pages in financial documents.

There is an absence of a dedicated benchmark for evaluating document parsing systems tailored to the extreme length, complex layouts, structural continuity, and high-precision requirements of the financial sector.
results: Agentar-Fin-OCR achieved a state-of-the-art Table TEDS score of 92.82 on OmniDocBench v1.5 and 95.7 on FinDocBench, outperforming leading specialized models in table parsing.

The Text+Image method for Document-Level Heading Hierarchy Reconstruction yielded an overall TocEDS score of 0.6273 on FinDocBench, showing up to +18.5% improvement on longer document types (e.g., Audit Reports).
The CellBBoxRegressor achieved a Mean IoU of 0.7199 and a Median IoU of 0.7555 on 572 financial tables in FinDocBench, providing reliable auditing-grade cell-level visual referencing.
solution: A multi-component framework integrates cross-page content consolidation and document-level heading hierarchy reconstruction using a 'pseudo-TOC' visual prompt and a VLM.

Table parsing is enhanced through curriculum learning and reinforcement optimization (GRPO), with training data stratified by structural complexity and inference consistency difficulty, and a reward function incorporating TEDS and grid-consistency.
A CellBBoxRegressor provides auditing-grade cell-level visual references by predicting bounding boxes directly from structural anchor tokens within the generated HTML stream.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Ant Group developed Agentar-Fin-OCR, a document parsing system that converts ultra-long financial PDFs into structured, auditable outputs by addressing semantic fragmentation and hierarchical disconnects. The system also introduces FinDocBench, a new benchmark tailored for evaluating document parsing capabilities within the financial sector.

title: Agentar-Fin-OCR

abstract: Effective document intelligence models rely on large amounts of annotated training data. However, procuring sufficient and high-quality data poses significant challenges due to the labor-intensive and costly nature of data acquisition. Additionally, leveraging language models to annotate real documents raises concerns about data privacy. Synthetic document generation has emerged as a promising, privacy-preserving alternative. We propose DocDjinn, a novel framework for controllable synthetic document generation using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that produces annotated documents from unlabeled seed samples. Our approach generates visually plausible and semantically consistent synthetic documents that follow the distribution of an existing source dataset through clustering-based seed selection with parametrized sampling. By enriching documents with realistic diffusion-based handwriting and contextual visual elements via semantic-visual decoupling, we generate diverse, high-quality annotated synthetic documents. We evaluate across eleven benchmarks spanning key information extraction, question answering, document classification, and document layout analysis. To our knowledge, this is the first work demonstrating that VLMs can generate faithful annotated document datasets at scale from unlabeled seeds that can effectively enrich or approximate real, manually annotated data for diverse document understanding tasks. We show that with only 100 real training samples, our framework achieves on average $87\%$ of the performance of the full real-world dataset. We publicly release our code and 140k+ synthetic document samples.

keyInsights: Intelligent seed selection, specifically intra-cluster sampling with a bias towards dominant patterns (α=1), effectively preserves structural coherence and improves synthetic data quality.
The synergistic combination of Vision-Language Models for semantic content and diffusion models for visual elements, like handwriting, yields highly realistic and functionally annotated documents.

Automating the generation of task-specific ground truth annotations within the synthetic document pipeline is critical for enabling direct supervised training and substantially reducing manual labeling efforts.
originalProblem: Acquiring large volumes of high-quality, annotated training data for document intelligence models is labor-intensive, costly, and sensitive to privacy concerns.
Existing synthetic data generation methods often lack textual coherence, multimodal realism, diverse content generation capabilities, or crucial task-specific ground truth annotations.
The scarcity of labeled data limits the scalability and adaptability of document understanding models, particularly for specialized domains or low-resource languages.
results: Models trained exclusively on DocDjinn-generated synthetic data achieved an average of 70.8% of the performance of models trained on full real datasets across eleven benchmarks.

Augmenting only 100 real labeled samples with synthetic data led to performance within 9.45 points (approximately 87%) of models trained on full real datasets, demonstrating a substantial reduction in annotation costs.

Full-shot augmentation (combining all real data with synthetic) matched or slightly exceeded real-only performance (average +0.51 points) on several tasks, indicating regularization benefits and enhanced model robustness.
DocDjinn achieved strong visual fidelity, with Layout-FID scores typically below 10 for most datasets, reflecting good distributional similarity to real documents.
solution: A four-stage pipeline is employed, beginning with intelligent seed sample selection using multimodal embeddings and clustering to align synthetic data distribution with real data.
Vision-Language Models synthesize HTML documents and automatically generate task-specific ground truth annotations (macro or micro formats) guided by detailed prompts.

Visual realism is enhanced by synthesizing realistic handwriting using a conditional latent diffusion model and by inserting contextual visual elements (stamps, barcodes, logos) sampled from image banks.
Final bounding boxes are extracted via OCR or PDF rendering, and the VLM-generated ground truth is rigorously verified against task-specific constraints to ensure annotation quality.
paper_summary.summary: DocDjinn presents a framework for generating high-fidelity, annotated synthetic documents by integrating Vision-Language Models for content and structure synthesis with diffusion models for realistic handwriting. The system automatically produces task-specific ground truth annotations, significantly reducing manual data labeling costs and enabling effective training of document intelligence models in data-scarce environments.

title: DocDjinn: Controllable Synthetic Document Generation with VLMs and Handwriting Diffusion

abstract:
Information in industry, research, and the public sector is widely stored as
rendered documents (e.g., PDF files, scans). Hence, to enable downstream tasks,
systems are needed that map rendered documents onto a structured hierarchical
format. However, existing systems for this task are limited by heuristics and
are not end-to-end trainable. In this work, we introduce the Document Structure
Generator (DSG), a novel system for document parsing that is fully end-to-end
trainable. DSG combines a deep neural network for parsing (i) entities in
documents (e.g., figures, text blocks, headers, etc.) and (ii) relations that
capture the sequence and nested structure between entities. Unlike existing
systems that rely on heuristics, our DSG is trained end-to-end, making it
effective and flexible for real-world applications. We further contribute a
new, large-scale dataset called E-Periodica comprising real-world magazines
with complex document structures for evaluation. Our results demonstrate that
our DSG outperforms commercial OCR tools and, on top of that, achieves
state-of-the-art performance. To the best of our knowledge, our DSG system is
the first end-to-end trainable system for hierarchical document parsing.
title: DSG: An End-to-End Document Structure Generator

abstract: Document pair extraction aims to identify key and value entities as well as their relationships from visually-rich documents. Most existing methods divide it into two separate tasks: semantic entity recognition (SER) and relation extraction (RE). However, simply concatenating SER and RE serially can lead to severe error propagation, and it fails to handle cases like multi-line entities in real scenarios. To address these issues, this paper introduces a novel framework, PEneo (Pair Extraction new decoder option), which performs document pair extraction in a unified pipeline, incorporating three concurrent sub-tasks: line extraction, line grouping, and entity linking. This approach alleviates the error accumulation problem and can handle the case of multi-line entities. Furthermore, to better evaluate the model's performance and to facilitate future research on pair extraction, we introduce RFUND, a re-annotated version of the commonly used FUNSD and XFUND datasets, to make them more accurate and cover realistic situations. Experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate PEneo's superiority over previous pipelines, boosting the performance by a large margin (e.g., 19.89%-22.91% F1 score on RFUND-EN) when combined with various backbones like LiLT and LayoutLMv3, showing its effectiveness and generality. Codes and the new annotations are available at this https URL.
title: PEneo: Unifying Line Extraction, Line Grouping, and Entity Linking for End-to-end Document Pair Extraction

abstract: Reading text from images or scanned documents via OCR models has been a longstanding focus of researchers. Intuitively, text reading is perceived as a straightforward perceptual task, and existing work primarily focuses on constructing enriched data engineering to enhance SFT capabilities. In this work, we observe that even advanced OCR models exhibit significantly higher entropy in formatted text (\emph{e.g.}, formula, table, etc.) compared to plain text, often by an order of magnitude. These statistical patterns reveal that advanced OCR models struggle with high output uncertainty when dealing with format sensitive document, suggesting that reasoning over diverse reading pathways may improve OCR performance. To address this, we propose format decoupled reinforcement learning (FD-RL), which leverages high-entropy patterns for targeted optimization. Our approach employs entropy-based data filtration strategy to identify format-intensive instances, and adopt format decoupled rewards tailored to different format types, enabling format-level validation rather than token-level memorization. FD-RL achieves an average score of 90.41 on OmniDocBench, setting a new record for end-to-end models on this highly popular benchmark. More importantly, we conduct comprehensive ablation studies over data, training, filtering, and rewarding strategies, thoroughly validating their effectiveness.

keyInsights: Document OCR, particularly for formatted content, fundamentally requires "reasoning over diverse reading pathways" beyond mere perceptual accuracy.
High output entropy in formatted text serves as a crucial indicator of underlying reasoning challenges, which can be effectively targeted and optimized by reinforcement learning.
Decoupling reward functions by specific content type (plain text, formulas, tables) allows models to learn robust format rules and significantly improve structural coherence and validity.
originalProblem: Advanced OCR models exhibit significantly higher output entropy and uncertainty when processing formatted text (e.g., formulas, tables) compared to plain text.
Existing supervised fine-tuning (SFT) approaches primarily achieve token-level memorization rather than format-level validation, especially for complex document structures.
Prior reinforcement learning (RL) attempts for OCR lacked a principled design to specifically target high-entropy formatted text and relied on empirically constructed datasets.
results: FD-RL achieved an overall score of 90.41 on OmniDocBench, setting a new state-of-the-art for end-to-end models and surpassing previous leading methods by up to 3.4 points.
The method established best performance in formula recognition (CDM 88.67) and table parsing (TEDS 87.35, TEDS-S 92.10) on the benchmark.

Ablation studies confirmed that reinforcement learning, entropy-based data filtration, and format-decoupled rewards each contribute substantially to performance gains, particularly improving formula recognition by +3.07 CDM and table parsing by +6.08 TEDS.
solution: The proposed Format Decoupled Reinforcement Learning (FD-RL) framework uses a two-stage SFT-then-RL training paradigm, built upon the Qwen3-VL-4B model and a large-scale, format-rich dataset.

An entropy-based data filtration strategy selects a subset of high-entropy samples (e.g., top 50%) for RL training, focusing optimization on instances with greater structural complexity and prediction uncertainty.
Format Decoupled Rewards are designed with content-specific metrics (normalized edit distance for text, BLEU for formulas, TEDS for tables) to optimize for structural validity and format correctness.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan researchers developed Format Decoupled Reinforcement Learning (FD-RL) to enhance document OCR, specifically addressing high output uncertainty in formatted content like formulas and tables. The method, which integrates supervised fine-tuning with targeted reinforcement learning, established a new state-of-the-art score of 90.41 on the OmniDocBench benchmark.

title: Reading or Reasoning? Format Decoupled Reinforcement Learning for Document OCR

abstract:
With the development of data-centric AI, the focus has shifted from
model-driven approaches to improving data quality. Academic literature, as one
of the crucial types, is predominantly stored in PDF formats and needs to be
parsed into texts before further processing. However, parsing diverse
structured texts in academic literature remains challenging due to the lack of
datasets that cover various text structures. In this paper, we introduce
AceParse, the first comprehensive dataset designed to support the parsing of a
wide range of structured texts, including formulas, tables, lists, algorithms,
and sentences with embedded mathematical expressions. Based on AceParse, we
fine-tuned a multimodal model, named AceParser, which accurately parses various
structured texts within academic literature. This model outperforms the
previous state-of-the-art by 4.1% in terms of F1 score and by 5% in Jaccard
Similarity, demonstrating the potential of multimodal models in academic
literature parsing. Our dataset is available at
this https URL
title: AceParse: A Comprehensive Dataset with Diverse Structured Texts for  Academic Literature Parsing

abstract: Document layout analysis is a known problem to the documents research community and has been vastly explored yielding a multitude of solutions ranging from text mining, and recognition to graph-based representation, visual feature extraction, etc. However, most of the existing works have ignored the crucial fact regarding the scarcity of labeled data. With growing internet connectivity to personal life, an enormous amount of documents had been available in the public domain and thus making data annotation a tedious task. We address this challenge using self-supervision and unlike, the few existing self-supervised document segmentation approaches which use text mining and textual labels, we use a complete vision-based approach in pre-training without any ground-truth label or its derivative. Instead, we generate pseudo-layouts from the document images to pre-train an image encoder to learn the document object representation and localization in a self-supervised framework before fine-tuning it with an object detection model. We show that our pipeline sets a new benchmark in this context and performs at par with the existing methods and the supervised counterparts, if not outperforms. The code is made publicly available at: this https URL
title: SelfDocSeg: A Self-Supervised vision-based Approach towards Document Segmentation

abstract: Medical document OCR is challenging due to complex layouts, domain-specific terminology, and noisy annotations, while requiring strict field-level exact matching. Existing OCR systems and general-purpose vision-language models often fail to reliably parse such documents. We propose MeDocVL, a post-trained vision-language model for query-driven medical document parsing. Our framework combines Training-driven Label Refinement to construct high-quality supervision from noisy annotations, with a Noise-aware Hybrid Post-training strategy that integrates reinforcement learning and supervised fine-tuning to achieve robust and precise extraction. Experiments on medical invoice benchmarks show that MeDocVL consistently outperforms conventional OCR systems and strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance under noisy supervision.
title: MeDocVL: A Visual Language Model for Medical Document Understanding and Parsing

abstract: Document parsing from scanned images into structured formats remains a significant challenge due to its complexly intertwined elements such as text paragraphs, figures, formulas, and tables. Existing supervised fine-tuning methods often struggle to generalize across diverse document types, leading to poor performance, particularly on out-of-distribution data. This issue is further exacerbated by the limited availability of high-quality training data for layout-aware parsing tasks. To address these challenges, we introduce LayoutRL, a reinforcement learning framework that optimizes layout understanding through composite rewards integrating normalized edit distance, paragraph count accuracy, and reading order preservation. To support this training, we construct the Infinity-Doc-400K dataset, which we use to train Infinity-Parser, a vision-language model demonstrating robust generalization across various domains. Extensive evaluations on benchmarks including OmniDocBench, olmOCR-Bench, PubTabNet, and FinTabNet show that Infinity-Parser consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across a broad range of document types, languages, and structural complexities, substantially outperforming both specialized document parsing systems and general-purpose vision-language models. We will release our code, dataset, and model to facilitate reproducible research in document parsing.
title: Infinity Parser: Layout Aware Reinforcement Learning for Scanned Document Parsing

abstract: Translating renderings (e. g. PDFs, scans) into hierarchical document structures is extensively demanded in the daily routines of many real-world applications. However, a holistic, principled approach to inferring the complete hierarchical structure of documents is missing. As a remedy, we developed "DocParser": an end-to-end system for parsing the complete document structure - including all text elements, nested figures, tables, and table cell structures. Our second contribution is to provide a dataset for evaluating hierarchical document structure parsing. Our third contribution is to propose a scalable learning framework for settings where domain-specific data are scarce, which we address by a novel approach to weak supervision that significantly improves the document structure parsing performance.
